About Career Roles

About Us

[Website Name] is a research website for studying, organizing, and explaining work in artificial intelligence, machine learning, AI systems, and future-facing technology. The goal of this website is to make research easier to read, revisit, and understand through structured notes, visual explanations, summaries, experiments, and original writing.

This website is inspired by research-focused projects like AI 2027, which present complex AI ideas through readable scenarios, supporting research, forecasts, and clear public documentation. In a similar spirit, [Website Name] aims to become a personal research archive and publication space for AI papers, technical ideas, project writeups, and independent analysis.

The website is created and maintained by Tejas Kashinath Jaware, a B.Tech Computer Science and Engineering student interested in AI/ML, computer vision, Generative AI, OCR systems, full-stack development, and applied AI research.

What this website is for

This website is being built as a public research workspace.

It will include:

  • Research paper summaries
  • Visual explanations of AI/ML concepts
  • Notes from papers, articles, and technical reports
  • My own research ideas and experiments
  • Project-based research writeups
  • AI/ML system diagrams
  • Timelines and changelogs of research progress
  • Eventually, my own research paper drafts and publications

The goal is not just to collect papers, but to understand them deeply, connect ideas across different fields, and explain them in a way that is useful for students, builders, and researchers.

Research Focus

The current focus of this website includes:

  • Machine Learning fundamentals
  • Computer Vision
  • OCR and document understanding
  • Generative AI and LLMs
  • Agentic AI systems
  • Model Context Protocol and AI tool use
  • Full-stack AI applications
  • Human-centered AI systems
  • AI safety, forecasting, and future scenarios
  • Applied AI for real-world problem solving

Some research pages will be technical, while others will be explanatory. The long-term aim is to build a structured knowledge base that grows with my learning, projects, and research work.

Original Research

This website will also document my own research and project-based experiments.

My current work includes SCRIPTIFY, an offline-first street sign transliteration system developed during Smart India Hackathon 2025. The project explores computer vision, OCR, transliteration, multilingual interfaces, and offline AI usability. SCRIPTIFY was selected as an SIH 2025 Grand Finalist, placing among the top approximately 0.5% teams nationally from over 54,000+ participating teams.

I am also exploring offline LLM-based processing, AI-native workflows, and applied AI systems through projects, internships, and independent study.

As the website evolves, original research pages will include:

  • Problem statements
  • Research questions
  • System architecture
  • Experiments
  • Visual diagrams
  • Failure cases
  • Limitations
  • Future work
  • Paper drafts and technical reports

Get in touch

You can reach me at tejusj98@gmail.com.

I welcome feedback, suggestions, collaboration ideas, and research discussions.

You can also find my work here:

  • GitHub
  • Portfolio
  • LinkedIn
  • YouTube

If you are interested in reviewing a research note, discussing an AI paper, or collaborating on an AI/ML project, feel free to reach out.

About the Author

Tejas Kashinath Jaware is a B.Tech Computer Science and Engineering student at Shri Sant Gadge Baba College of Engineering & Technology, Bhusawal. His interests include machine learning, computer vision, OCR systems, Generative AI, full-stack software development, agentic AI workflows, and applied AI research.

He was the sole developer and technical lead for SCRIPTIFY, an offline-first street sign transliteration app built during Smart India Hackathon 2025, where the project reached the Grand Finale. He is also a Google Gemini Student Ambassador 2026 and an Applied AI Intern at 1M1B, working on AI/ML workflows for real-world problem statements.

Through this website, he is building a public research archive to document what he reads, builds, experiments with, and learns.

How the research is organized

Research on this website will be organized into several types of pages:

Summaries

Short explanations of important AI/ML papers and technical reports.

Research Notes

Longer notes that explain ideas, methods, assumptions, and implications.

Visual Explainers

Diagrams, timelines, flowcharts, and interactive visuals that make complex ideas easier to understand.

Experiments

Project-based experiments, implementation notes, model tests, system designs, and failure cases.

Original Papers

Drafts, preprints, or final versions of my own research papers and technical reports.

Changelog

A record of updates made to the website, research notes, visualizations, and paper drafts.

Contributions

The website, research notes, visual explanations, and original writing are created and maintained by Tejas Kashinath Jaware.

Some pages may summarize or explain work originally published by researchers, labs, universities, companies, or open-source communities. Such pages will cite and link to the original sources wherever possible.

This website is a learning and research documentation project. It is not meant to replace original papers, but to make them easier to understand, organize, and revisit.

Acknowledgements

This website is inspired by public research projects and explainers such as AI 2027, AI forecasting work, open-source AI communities, academic papers, and educational resources that make technical knowledge more accessible.

Special thanks to:

  • Researchers and authors who publish open AI/ML papers
  • Open-source communities building AI tools and frameworks
  • Smart India Hackathon 2025 for shaping my project-based research mindset
  • Google Gemini Student Ambassador Program for AI-first development exposure
  • 1M1B Applied AI initiative for applied AI and sustainability-focused learning
  • AICTE IDE Bootcamp and Wadhwani Foundation mentors for product and research direction

Changelog

We are committed to continuously improving the SCRIPTIFY Platform. Keep track of all updates, bug fixes, and new features right here.

Version 1.1 (Current)

Date: February 14, 2024

Description: Second iteration with UI/UX improvements, backend integration, and the addition of core features.

Key Updates:
  • ✓ Redesigned dashboard with real-time updates.
  • ✓ Integrated AI capabilities for automated reporting.
  • ✓ Improved performance and loading times.
  • ✓ Expanded user settings and preferences.

Version 1.0

Date: January 21, 2024

Description: Initial release. Focused on core functionality and user onboarding.

Key Updates:
  • ✓ Basic user authentication and profiles.
  • ✓ Initial feature set implemented.
  • ✓ Foundational database architecture set up.

Future updates

Planned updates include:

  • Adding the first research paper summary
  • Creating visual explanations for selected AI/ML concepts
  • Publishing SCRIPTIFY technical writeup
  • Adding a reading list for important AI/ML papers
  • Adding diagrams for OCR and LLM-based workflows
  • Creating original research paper drafts
  • Adding citation and reference sections

Jobs & Career Roles

A practical guide to future-relevant roles: what they involve, how to prepare, how long learning may take, and how AI may change the work.

Note: This is a career research note, not a job board. Always verify details from official sources.

Anthropic Fellows Program — Overview

Read guide

AI research fellowship across safety, security, ML systems, RL, economics, and policy. In practical terms, this role is about turning ambiguous AI problems into working systems, experiments, evaluations, tools, or research artifacts. It is not only about knowing AI concepts; it requires building, measuring, debugging, writing, and making decisions under uncertainty.

Prepare: 12–24 months · Salary: ~$3,850/week US; £2,310/week UK; C$4,300/week Canada · Risk: Low–Medium

Anthropic Fellows Program — Overview

Prepare: 12–24 months
Salary: ~$3,850/week US; £2,310/week UK; C$4,300/week Canada
Risk: Low–Medium

AI research fellowship across safety, security, ML systems, RL, economics, and policy. In practical terms, this role is about turning ambiguous AI problems into working systems, experiments, evaluations, tools, or research artifacts. It is not only about knowing AI concepts; it requires building, measuring, debugging, writing, and making decisions under uncertainty. For a student, the most important thing to understand is that this role rewards proof of ability: projects, research notes, reproducible experiments, open-source contributions, clear writing, and evidence that you can work through hard problems without needing perfect instructions.

Roles like this exist because advanced AI systems are becoming large, complex, and socially important. They need people who can improve capability while also thinking about reliability, safety, correctness, interpretability, performance, and real-world impact. AI can help with code and analysis, but high-stakes AI work still needs humans who can decide what to measure, which failures matter, and what should be trusted. By 2050, many routine tasks inside this role may be automated. The role may still remain valuable because the hard part is not only generating code or text; it is choosing good questions, designing robust systems, interpreting results, and taking responsibility for outcomes.

What you do

  • Translate ambiguous research/product/systems goals into concrete technical work.
  • Build experiments, infrastructure, evaluations, dashboards, tools, or model pipelines.
  • Debug failures across models, data, infrastructure, and human workflows.
  • Communicate findings through technical writing, design docs, reports, or presentations.
  • Collaborate with researchers, engineers, product teams, safety teams, or external experts.
  • Maintain high standards for reliability, reproducibility, and responsible deployment.
  • Use AI tools as accelerators while still verifying outputs and making final judgments.

Preparation Timeline

Month 0–3 — Foundations
Learn Python, Git/GitHub, Linux CLI, basic math, and how to read technical documentation. Build 2–3 small public repositories.
Month 3–6 — Applied ML and systems basics
Learn ML fundamentals, model evaluation, APIs, data handling, and basic backend or notebook workflows. Build one reproducible ML/evaluation project.
Month 6–12 — Role-specific projects
Pick one subfield and build a focused project: benchmark, dashboard, model-serving app, RL environment, safety classifier, interpretability notebook, or data pipeline.
Month 12–24 — Public proof of ability
Reproduce a small paper/blog result, publish a technical writeup, improve the project quality, and create a portfolio page with clear motivation, method, results, and limitations.
Beyond 24 months — Specialization
Deepen one track through internships, open-source work, research collaborations, production projects, or advanced systems/research projects.

Reality Check

This role is competitive. It is usually not an immediate beginner role unless it is explicitly an internship or fellowship. A first-year student should treat it as a long-term target and build toward it through small public projects, then larger reproducible projects, then collaborations, internships, research-style writing, or production-quality systems. Do not only collect certificates. For this kind of role, visible proof matters more: GitHub repositories, demos, benchmarks, technical writeups, open-source contributions, and evidence that you can finish hard projects.

Core Skills

Linux + systems debugging
Operate and debug production-like systems
Distributed systems
Scale training/inference across machines
Docker/Kubernetes
Run reproducible services and training jobs
Performance profiling
Find bottlenecks in code and infrastructure
Additional cross-cutting skills for this role: - Research writing: explain motivation, method, results, limitations, and next steps. - Experiment design: avoid misleading conclusions from noisy results. - Communication: make technical work understandable to teammates and stakeholders. - AI-assisted workflow: use AI to move faster, but verify everything important.

Proof of Work Projects

Project Level Time Expected Output
Model-serving API with load test Intermediate 3–6 weeks API + latency report
Distributed training toy demo Advanced 6–10 weeks Repo + writeup
Observability dashboard Intermediate 3–6 weeks Dashboard + metrics
A strong project for this role should include: 1. A clear problem statement. 2. A reproducible GitHub repository. 3. A short writeup explaining what you tried. 4. Results, screenshots, logs, benchmarks, or evaluation data. 5. A section on limitations and what you would improve next.

What AI Automation Replaces

  • Boilerplate code generation.
  • First drafts of documentation and reports.
  • Literature and documentation summaries.
  • Simple debugging suggestions.
  • Test case generation.
  • Plot/table generation.
  • Basic data cleaning or log summarization.
  • Scaffolding for APIs, dashboards, or experiments.

Why Humans Matter

  • Choosing the right problem is harder than generating code.
  • AI-generated experiments can be misleading without human judgment.
  • Safety, correctness, and reliability require accountability.
  • Real systems fail in messy ways that need context and ownership.
  • Research taste and product taste are difficult to automate fully.
  • Cross-team trust, prioritization, and communication remain human-heavy.
  • Someone must decide what evidence is strong enough to act on.

Best Next Step

Pick one small project from the table above. Build it in public. Write a clear README with: problem, method, results, limitations, and next steps. Then improve it once based on your own critique.

Official Source Links

  • Official Anthropic job board
  • Anthropic careers
  • Candidate AI usage guidance
  • Alignment Science blog
  • Frontier Red Team blog
  • Transformer Circuits
Back to Directory

AI Safety Fellows

Prepare: 12–24 months
Salary: ~$3,850/week US; £2,310/week UK; C$4,300/week Canada
Risk: Low–Medium

Empirical AI safety research: scalable oversight, AI control, model organisms, interpretability, AI welfare. In practical terms, this role is about turning ambiguous AI problems into working systems, experiments, evaluations, tools, or research artifacts. It is not only about knowing AI concepts; it requires building, measuring, debugging, writing, and making decisions under uncertainty. For a student, the most important thing to understand is that this role rewards proof of ability: projects, research notes, reproducible experiments, open-source contributions, clear writing, and evidence that you can work through hard problems without needing perfect instructions.

Roles like this exist because advanced AI systems are becoming large, complex, and socially important. They need people who can improve capability while also thinking about reliability, safety, correctness, interpretability, performance, and real-world impact. AI can help with code and analysis, but high-stakes AI work still needs humans who can decide what to measure, which failures matter, and what should be trusted. By 2050, many routine tasks inside this role may be automated. The role may still remain valuable because the hard part is not only generating code or text; it is choosing good questions, designing robust systems, interpreting results, and taking responsibility for outcomes.

What you do

  • Translate ambiguous research/product/systems goals into concrete technical work.
  • Build experiments, infrastructure, evaluations, dashboards, tools, or model pipelines.
  • Debug failures across models, data, infrastructure, and human workflows.
  • Communicate findings through technical writing, design docs, reports, or presentations.
  • Collaborate with researchers, engineers, product teams, safety teams, or external experts.
  • Maintain high standards for reliability, reproducibility, and responsible deployment.
  • Use AI tools as accelerators while still verifying outputs and making final judgments.

Preparation Timeline

Month 0–3 — Foundations
Learn Python, Git/GitHub, Linux CLI, basic math, and how to read technical documentation. Build 2–3 small public repositories.
Month 3–6 — Applied ML and systems basics
Learn ML fundamentals, model evaluation, APIs, data handling, and basic backend or notebook workflows. Build one reproducible ML/evaluation project.
Month 6–12 — Role-specific projects
Pick one subfield and build a focused project: benchmark, dashboard, model-serving app, RL environment, safety classifier, interpretability notebook, or data pipeline.
Month 12–24 — Public proof of ability
Reproduce a small paper/blog result, publish a technical writeup, improve the project quality, and create a portfolio page with clear motivation, method, results, and limitations.
Beyond 24 months — Specialization
Deepen one track through internships, open-source work, research collaborations, production projects, or advanced systems/research projects.

Reality Check

This role is competitive. It is usually not an immediate beginner role unless it is explicitly an internship or fellowship. A first-year student should treat it as a long-term target and build toward it through small public projects, then larger reproducible projects, then collaborations, internships, research-style writing, or production-quality systems. Do not only collect certificates. For this kind of role, visible proof matters more: GitHub repositories, demos, benchmarks, technical writeups, open-source contributions, and evidence that you can finish hard projects.

Core Skills

Linux + systems debugging
Operate and debug production-like systems
Distributed systems
Scale training/inference across machines
Docker/Kubernetes
Run reproducible services and training jobs
Performance profiling
Find bottlenecks in code and infrastructure
Additional cross-cutting skills for this role: - Research writing: explain motivation, method, results, limitations, and next steps. - Experiment design: avoid misleading conclusions from noisy results. - Communication: make technical work understandable to teammates and stakeholders. - AI-assisted workflow: use AI to move faster, but verify everything important.

Proof of Work Projects

Project Level Time Expected Output
Model-serving API with load test Intermediate 3–6 weeks API + latency report
Distributed training toy demo Advanced 6–10 weeks Repo + writeup
Observability dashboard Intermediate 3–6 weeks Dashboard + metrics
A strong project for this role should include: 1. A clear problem statement. 2. A reproducible GitHub repository. 3. A short writeup explaining what you tried. 4. Results, screenshots, logs, benchmarks, or evaluation data. 5. A section on limitations and what you would improve next.

What AI Automation Replaces

  • Boilerplate code generation.
  • First drafts of documentation and reports.
  • Literature and documentation summaries.
  • Simple debugging suggestions.
  • Test case generation.
  • Plot/table generation.
  • Basic data cleaning or log summarization.
  • Scaffolding for APIs, dashboards, or experiments.

Why Humans Matter

  • Choosing the right problem is harder than generating code.
  • AI-generated experiments can be misleading without human judgment.
  • Safety, correctness, and reliability require accountability.
  • Real systems fail in messy ways that need context and ownership.
  • Research taste and product taste are difficult to automate fully.
  • Cross-team trust, prioritization, and communication remain human-heavy.
  • Someone must decide what evidence is strong enough to act on.

Best Next Step

Pick one small project from the table above. Build it in public. Write a clear README with: problem, method, results, limitations, and next steps. Then improve it once based on your own critique.

  • Official Anthropic job board
  • Anthropic careers

  • Alignment Science blog
  • Frontier Red Team blog
  • Transformer Circuits

AI Security Fellows

Prepare: 12–24 months
Salary: ~$3,850/week US; £2,310/week UK; C$4,300/week Canada
Risk: Low

AI security, red teaming, vulnerability research, LLM security, control evaluations, adversarial testing. In practical terms, this role is about turning ambiguous AI problems into working systems, experiments, evaluations, tools, or research artifacts. It is not only about knowing AI concepts; it requires building, measuring, debugging, writing, and making decisions under uncertainty. For a student, the most important thing to understand is that this role rewards proof of ability: projects, research notes, reproducible experiments, open-source contributions, clear writing, and evidence that you can work through hard problems without needing perfect instructions.

Roles like this exist because advanced AI systems are becoming large, complex, and socially important. They need people who can improve capability while also thinking about reliability, safety, correctness, interpretability, performance, and real-world impact. AI can help with code and analysis, but high-stakes AI work still needs humans who can decide what to measure, which failures matter, and what should be trusted. By 2050, many routine tasks inside this role may be automated. The role may still remain valuable because the hard part is not only generating code or text; it is choosing good questions, designing robust systems, interpreting results, and taking responsibility for outcomes.

What you do

  • Translate ambiguous research/product/systems goals into concrete technical work.
  • Build experiments, infrastructure, evaluations, dashboards, tools, or model pipelines.
  • Debug failures across models, data, infrastructure, and human workflows.
  • Communicate findings through technical writing, design docs, reports, or presentations.
  • Collaborate with researchers, engineers, product teams, safety teams, or external experts.
  • Maintain high standards for reliability, reproducibility, and responsible deployment.
  • Use AI tools as accelerators while still verifying outputs and making final judgments.

Preparation Timeline

Month 0–3 — Foundations
Learn Python, Git/GitHub, Linux CLI, basic math, and how to read technical documentation. Build 2–3 small public repositories.
Month 3–6 — Applied ML and systems basics
Learn ML fundamentals, model evaluation, APIs, data handling, and basic backend or notebook workflows. Build one reproducible ML/evaluation project.
Month 6–12 — Role-specific projects
Pick one subfield and build a focused project: benchmark, dashboard, model-serving app, RL environment, safety classifier, interpretability notebook, or data pipeline.
Month 12–24 — Public proof of ability
Reproduce a small paper/blog result, publish a technical writeup, improve the project quality, and create a portfolio page with clear motivation, method, results, and limitations.
Beyond 24 months — Specialization
Deepen one track through internships, open-source work, research collaborations, production projects, or advanced systems/research projects.

Reality Check

This role is competitive. It is usually not an immediate beginner role unless it is explicitly an internship or fellowship. A first-year student should treat it as a long-term target and build toward it through small public projects, then larger reproducible projects, then collaborations, internships, research-style writing, or production-quality systems. Do not only collect certificates. For this kind of role, visible proof matters more: GitHub repositories, demos, benchmarks, technical writeups, open-source contributions, and evidence that you can finish hard projects.

Core Skills

Python
Build evaluations and analysis pipelines
LLM evaluation
Measure honesty, safety, and model behavior
Statistics
Interpret noisy evaluation results
AI safety basics
Understand alignment, control, oversight, and failure modes
Additional cross-cutting skills for this role: - Research writing: explain motivation, method, results, limitations, and next steps. - Experiment design: avoid misleading conclusions from noisy results. - Communication: make technical work understandable to teammates and stakeholders. - AI-assisted workflow: use AI to move faster, but verify everything important.

Proof of Work Projects

Project Level Time Expected Output
LLM honesty benchmark Beginner–Intermediate 1–3 weeks Dataset + report
Prompt injection test suite Intermediate 2–4 weeks Tests + findings
Safety classifier prototype Intermediate 4–8 weeks Classifier + eval report
A strong project for this role should include: 1. A clear problem statement. 2. A reproducible GitHub repository. 3. A short writeup explaining what you tried. 4. Results, screenshots, logs, benchmarks, or evaluation data. 5. A section on limitations and what you would improve next.

What AI Automation Replaces

  • Boilerplate code generation.
  • First drafts of documentation and reports.
  • Literature and documentation summaries.
  • Simple debugging suggestions.
  • Test case generation.
  • Plot/table generation.
  • Basic data cleaning or log summarization.
  • Scaffolding for APIs, dashboards, or experiments.

Why Humans Matter

  • Choosing the right problem is harder than generating code.
  • AI-generated experiments can be misleading without human judgment.
  • Safety, correctness, and reliability require accountability.
  • Real systems fail in messy ways that need context and ownership.
  • Research taste and product taste are difficult to automate fully.
  • Cross-team trust, prioritization, and communication remain human-heavy.
  • Someone must decide what evidence is strong enough to act on.

Best Next Step

Pick one small project from the table above. Build it in public. Write a clear README with: problem, method, results, limitations, and next steps. Then improve it once based on your own critique.

ML Systems & Performance Fellows

Prepare: 12–30 months
Salary: ~$3,850/week US; £2,310/week UK; C$4,300/week Canada
Risk: Low

Engineering-heavy ML systems, accelerator workloads, infrastructure, performance, distributed pipelines. In practical terms, this role is about turning ambiguous AI problems into working systems, experiments, evaluations, tools, or research artifacts. It is not only about knowing AI concepts; it requires building, measuring, debugging, writing, and making decisions under uncertainty. For a student, the most important thing to understand is that this role rewards proof of ability: projects, research notes, reproducible experiments, open-source contributions, clear writing, and evidence that you can work through hard problems without needing perfect instructions.

Roles like this exist because advanced AI systems are becoming large, complex, and socially important. They need people who can improve capability while also thinking about reliability, safety, correctness, interpretability, performance, and real-world impact. AI can help with code and analysis, but high-stakes AI work still needs humans who can decide what to measure, which failures matter, and what should be trusted. By 2050, many routine tasks inside this role may be automated. The role may still remain valuable because the hard part is not only generating code or text; it is choosing good questions, designing robust systems, interpreting results, and taking responsibility for outcomes.

What you do

  • Translate ambiguous research/product/systems goals into concrete technical work.
  • Build experiments, infrastructure, evaluations, dashboards, tools, or model pipelines.
  • Debug failures across models, data, infrastructure, and human workflows.
  • Communicate findings through technical writing, design docs, reports, or presentations.
  • Collaborate with researchers, engineers, product teams, safety teams, or external experts.
  • Maintain high standards for reliability, reproducibility, and responsible deployment.
  • Use AI tools as accelerators while still verifying outputs and making final judgments.

Preparation Timeline

Month 0–3 — Foundations
Learn Python, Git/GitHub, Linux CLI, basic math, and how to read technical documentation. Build 2–3 small public repositories.

Learn ML fundamentals, model evaluation, APIs, data handling, and basic backend or notebook workflows. Build one reproducible ML/evaluation project.
Month 6–12 — Role-specific projects
Pick one subfield and build a focused project: benchmark, dashboard, model-serving app, RL environment, safety classifier, interpretability notebook, or data pipeline.
Month 12–24 — Public proof of ability
Reproduce a small paper/blog result, publish a technical writeup, improve the project quality, and create a portfolio page with clear motivation, method, results, and limitations.
Beyond 24 months — Specialization
Deepen one track through internships, open-source work, research collaborations, production projects, or advanced systems/research projects.

Reality Check

This role is competitive. It is usually not an immediate beginner role unless it is explicitly an internship or fellowship. A first-year student should treat it as a long-term target and build toward it through small public projects, then larger reproducible projects, then collaborations, internships, research-style writing, or production-quality systems. Do not only collect certificates. For this kind of role, visible proof matters more: GitHub repositories, demos, benchmarks, technical writeups, open-source contributions, and evidence that you can finish hard projects.

Core Skills

CUDA/Triton basics
Write and optimize kernels
GPU architecture
Understand memory bandwidth, tensor cores, and occupancy
Profiling tools
Use Nsight/profilers to locate bottlenecks
Low precision inference
Understand FP8/INT8, quantization, and numerical tradeoffs
Additional cross-cutting skills for this role: - Research writing: explain motivation, method, results, limitations, and next steps. - Experiment design: avoid misleading conclusions from noisy results. - Communication: make technical work understandable to teammates and stakeholders. - AI-assisted workflow: use AI to move faster, but verify everything important.

Proof of Work Projects

Project Level Time Expected Output
CUDA matrix multiplication Intermediate 3–6 weeks Kernel + benchmark
Triton attention microbenchmark Advanced 4–8 weeks Benchmark + report
Quantized inference experiment Advanced 4–8 weeks Accuracy/latency analysis
A strong project for this role should include: 1. A clear problem statement. 2. A reproducible GitHub repository. 3. A short writeup explaining what you tried. 4. Results, screenshots, logs, benchmarks, or evaluation data. 5. A section on limitations and what you would improve next.

What AI Automation Replaces

  • Boilerplate code generation.
  • First drafts of documentation and reports.
  • Literature and documentation summaries.
  • Simple debugging suggestions.
  • Test case generation.
  • Plot/table generation.
  • Basic data cleaning or log summarization.
  • Scaffolding for APIs, dashboards, or experiments.

Why Humans Matter

  • Choosing the right problem is harder than generating code.
  • AI-generated experiments can be misleading without human judgment.
  • Safety, correctness, and reliability require accountability.
  • Real systems fail in messy ways that need context and ownership.
  • Research taste and product taste are difficult to automate fully.
  • Cross-team trust, prioritization, and communication remain human-heavy.
  • Someone must decide what evidence is strong enough to act on.

Reinforcement Learning Fellows

Prepare: 12–24 months
Salary: ~$3,850/week US; £2,310/week UK; C$4,300/week Canada
Risk: Low

RL algorithms, training data quality, environment design, generalization, safety-related RL tasks. In practical terms, this role is about turning ambiguous AI problems into working systems, experiments, evaluations, tools, or research artifacts. It is not only about knowing AI concepts; it requires building, measuring, debugging, writing, and making decisions under uncertainty. For a student, the most important thing to understand is that this role rewards proof of ability: projects, research notes, reproducible experiments, open-source contributions, clear writing, and evidence that you can work through hard problems without needing perfect instructions.

Roles like this exist because advanced AI systems are becoming large, complex, and socially important. AI can help with code and analysis, but high-stakes AI work still needs humans who can decide what to measure, which failures matter, and what should be trusted. By 2050, many routine tasks inside this role may be automated. The role may still remain valuable because the hard part is not only generating code or text; it is choosing good questions, designing robust systems, interpreting results, and taking responsibility for outcomes.

  • Build experiments, infrastructure, evaluations, dashboards, tools, or model pipelines.
  • Debug failures across models, data, infrastructure, and human workflows.
  • Communicate findings through technical writing, design docs, reports, or presentations.

Pick one subfield and build a focused project: benchmark, dashboard, model-serving app, RL environment, safety classifier, interpretability notebook, or data pipeline.

Python async/concurrency
Build fast research pipelines and environments
RL fundamentals

PyTorch/JAX
Implement and debug model training
Evaluation design
Measure agent behavior reliably
Additional cross-cutting skills for this role: - Research writing: explain motivation, method, results, limitations, and next steps. - Experiment design: avoid misleading conclusions from noisy results. - Communication: make technical work understandable to teammates and stakeholders. - AI-assisted workflow: use AI to move faster, but verify everything important.

Proof of Work Projects

Project Level Time Expected Output
Gridworld RL environment Beginner–Intermediate 1–2 weeks Environment + notes
Code-verifier benchmark Intermediate 3–6 weeks Verifier + tasks
Long-horizon agent sandbox Advanced 6–12 weeks Sandbox + report
A strong project for this role should include: 1. A clear problem statement. 2. A reproducible GitHub repository. 3. A short writeup explaining what you tried. 4. Results, screenshots, logs, benchmarks, or evaluation data. 5. A section on limitations and what you would improve next.

What AI Automation Replaces

  • Boilerplate code generation.
  • First drafts of documentation and reports.

  • Simple debugging suggestions.
  • Test case generation.
  • Plot/table generation.
  • Basic data cleaning or log summarization.
  • Scaffolding for APIs, dashboards, or experiments.

Why Humans Matter

  • Choosing the right problem is harder than generating code.

  • Safety, correctness, and reliability require accountability.

  • Research taste and product taste are difficult to automate fully.
  • Cross-team trust, prioritization, and communication remain human-heavy.

Economics & Policy Fellows

Salary: ~$3,850/week US; £2,310/week UK; C$4,300/week Canada
Risk: Low–Medium

Design Engineer, AI Capability Development — Education Labs

Prepare: 24–48 months
Salary: $300k–$405k USD
Risk: Low–Medium

Hybrid full-stack, product design, interaction design, and research-minded role for AI experiences that increase human capability. In practical terms, this role is about turning ambiguous AI problems into working systems, experiments, evaluations, tools, or research artifacts. Reality Check

This role is competitive. It is usually not an immediate beginner role unless it is explicitly an internship or fellowship. A first-year student should treat it as a long-term target and build toward it through small public projects, then larger reproducible projects, then collaborations, internships, research-style writing, or production-quality systems. Do not only collect certificates. For this kind of role, visible proof matters more: GitHub repositories, demos, benchmarks, technical writeups, open-source contributions, and evidence that you can finish hard projects.

Core Skills

Full-stack engineering
Own product from database to interface
React/TypeScript
Build polished user-facing tools
Product judgment
Decide what to build and what to cut
LLM APIs/agent frameworks
Build AI-native experiences
Additional cross-cutting skills for this role: - Research writing: explain motivation, method, results, limitations, and next steps. - Experiment design: avoid misleading conclusions from noisy results. - Communication: make technical work understandable to teammates and stakeholders. - AI-assisted workflow: use AI to move faster, but verify everything important.

Proof of Work Projects

Project Level Time Expected Output
AI workflow product Intermediate 3–6 weeks Deployed app + case study
Research dashboard Intermediate 3–6 weeks Dashboard + users
Browser-control agent prototype Advanced 6–10 weeks Agent + eval notes
A strong project for this role should include: 1. A clear problem statement. 2. A reproducible GitHub repository. 3. A short writeup explaining what you tried. 4. Results, screenshots, logs, benchmarks, or evaluation data. 5. A section on limitations and what you would improve next.

What AI Automation Replaces

  • Boilerplate code generation.
  • First drafts of documentation and reports.
  • Literature and documentation summaries.
  • Simple debugging suggestions.
  • Test case generation.
  • Plot/table generation.
  • Basic data cleaning or log summarization.
  • Scaffolding for APIs, dashboards, or experiments.

Why Humans Matter

  • Choosing the right problem is harder than generating code.
  • AI-generated experiments can be misleading without human judgment.
  • Safety, correctness, and reliability require accountability.
  • Real systems fail in messy ways that need context and ownership.
  • Research taste and product taste are difficult to automate fully.
  • Cross-team trust, prioritization, and communication remain human-heavy.
  • Someone must decide what evidence is strong enough to act on.

Best Next Step

Pick one small project from the table above. Build it in public. Write a clear README with: problem, method, results, limitations, and next steps. Then improve it once based on your own critique.

Official Source Links

  • Official Anthropic job board
  • Anthropic careers
  • Candidate AI usage guidance
  • Alignment Science blog
  • Frontier Red Team blog
  • Transformer Circuits
Back to Directory

Engineering Manager, GPU — ML Accelerator

Prepare: 5–10+ years
Salary: $500k–$850k USD
Risk: Low

Technical leadership for GPU/accelerator performance, training/inference scale, bottleneck removal, and engineering execution. In practical terms, this role is about turning ambiguous AI problems into working systems, experiments, evaluations, tools, or research artifacts. It is not only about knowing AI concepts; it requires building, measuring, debugging, writing, and making decisions under uncertainty. For a student, the most important thing to understand is that this role rewards proof of ability: projects, research notes, reproducible experiments, open-source contributions, clear writing, and evidence that you can work through hard problems without needing perfect instructions.

Roles like this exist because advanced AI systems are becoming large, complex, and socially important. They need people who can improve capability while also thinking about reliability, safety, correctness, interpretability, performance, and real-world impact. AI can help with code and analysis, but high-stakes AI work still needs humans who can decide what to measure, which failures matter, and what should be trusted. By 2050, many routine tasks inside this role may be automated. The role may still remain valuable because the hard part is not only generating code or text; it is choosing good questions, designing robust systems, interpreting results, and taking responsibility for outcomes.

What you do

  • Translate ambiguous research/product/systems goals into concrete technical work.
  • Build experiments, infrastructure, evaluations, dashboards, tools, or model pipelines.
  • Debug failures across models, data, infrastructure, and human workflows.
  • Communicate findings through technical writing, design docs, reports, or presentations.
  • Collaborate with researchers, engineers, product teams, safety teams, or external experts.
  • Maintain high standards for reliability, reproducibility, and responsible deployment.
  • Use AI tools as accelerators while still verifying outputs and making final judgments.

Preparation Timeline

Year 1 — Core foundations
Learn Python deeply, Git/GitHub, Linux CLI, data structures, debugging, and basic ML concepts. Build small but clean projects and write about them.
Year 2 — Serious technical projects
Choose a direction and build projects that require reliability: experiments, dashboards, benchmarks, data pipelines, model-serving APIs, or systems tools. Start reading papers and technical blogs.
Years 3–4 — Production-style proof of work
Build larger projects with tests, documentation, monitoring, and clear design decisions. Contribute to open source or collaborate with researchers/builders. Publish technical writeups.
Years 4–7 — Specialization and ownership
Own a domain deeply: interpretability, ML systems, GPU performance, AI safety, RL, product engineering, or data infrastructure. Show evidence that you can take ambiguous problems to concrete results.
Years 7–10+ — Leadership or senior research track
For manager/lead roles, build a record of mentoring people, setting direction, recruiting, prioritizing projects, and communicating technical strategy. For senior IC roles, build a public record of high-impact systems or research contributions.

Reality Check

This role is competitive. It is usually not an immediate beginner role unless it is explicitly an internship or fellowship. A first-year student should treat it as a long-term target and build toward it through small public projects, then larger reproducible projects, then collaborations, internships, research-style writing, or production-quality systems. Do not only collect certificates. For this kind of role, visible proof matters more: GitHub repositories, demos, benchmarks, technical writeups, open-source contributions, and evidence that you can finish hard projects.

Core Skills

CUDA/Triton basics
Write and optimize kernels
GPU architecture
Understand memory bandwidth, tensor cores, and occupancy
Profiling tools
Use Nsight/profilers to locate bottlenecks
Low precision inference
Understand FP8/INT8, quantization, and numerical tradeoffs
Additional cross-cutting skills for this role: - Research writing: explain motivation, method, results, limitations, and next steps. - Experiment design: avoid misleading conclusions from noisy results. - Communication: make technical work understandable to teammates and stakeholders. - AI-assisted workflow: use AI to move faster, but verify everything important.

Proof of Work Projects

Project Level Time Expected Output
CUDA matrix multiplication Intermediate 3–6 weeks Kernel + benchmark
Triton attention microbenchmark Advanced 4–8 weeks Benchmark + report
Quantized inference experiment Advanced 4–8 weeks Accuracy/latency analysis
A strong project for this role should include: 1. A clear problem statement. 2. A reproducible GitHub repository. 3. A short writeup explaining what you tried. 4. Results, screenshots, logs, benchmarks, or evaluation data. 5. A section on limitations and what you would improve next.

What AI Automation Replaces

  • Boilerplate code generation.
  • First drafts of documentation and reports.
  • Literature and documentation summaries.
  • Simple debugging suggestions.
  • Test case generation.
  • Plot/table generation.
  • Basic data cleaning or log summarization.
  • Scaffolding for APIs, dashboards, or experiments.

Why Humans Matter

  • Choosing the right problem is harder than generating code.
  • AI-generated experiments can be misleading without human judgment.
  • Safety, correctness, and reliability require accountability.
  • Real systems fail in messy ways that need context and ownership.
  • Research taste and product taste are difficult to automate fully.
  • Cross-team trust, prioritization, and communication remain human-heavy.
  • Someone must decide what evidence is strong enough to act on.

Best Next Step

Pick one small project from the table above. Build it in public. Write a clear README with: problem, method, results, limitations, and next steps. Then improve it once based on your own critique.

Salary: $425k–$560k USD

In practical terms, this role is about turning ambiguous AI problems into working systems, experiments, evaluations, tools, or research artifacts. It is not only about knowing AI concepts; it requires building, measuring, debugging, writing, and making decisions under uncertainty. For a student, the most important thing to understand is that this role rewards proof of ability: projects, research notes, reproducible experiments, open-source contributions, clear writing, and evidence that you can work through hard problems without needing perfect instructions.

Roles like this exist because advanced AI systems are becoming large, complex, and socially important. They need people who can improve capability while also thinking about reliability, safety, correctness, interpretability, performance, and real-world impact. AI can help with code and analysis, but high-stakes AI work still needs humans who can decide what to measure, which failures matter, and what should be trusted. By 2050, many routine tasks inside this role may be automated. The role may still remain valuable because the hard part is not only generating code or text; it is choosing good questions, designing robust systems, interpreting results, and taking responsibility for outcomes.

Do not only collect certificates. CUDA/Triton basics

Proof of Work Projects

Project Level Time Expected Output
CUDA matrix multiplication Intermediate 3–6 weeks Kernel + benchmark
Triton attention microbenchmark Advanced 4–8 weeks Benchmark + report

What AI Automation Replaces

  • Boilerplate code generation.
  • First drafts of documentation and reports.
  • Literature and documentation summaries.
  • Simple debugging suggestions.
  • Test case generation.
  • Plot/table generation.
  • Basic data cleaning or log summarization.
  • Scaffolding for APIs, dashboards, or experiments.

  • Safety, correctness, and reliability require accountability.

For a student, the most important thing to understand is that this role rewards proof of ability: projects, research notes, reproducible experiments, open-source contributions, clear writing, and evidence that you can work through hard problems without needing perfect instructions.

Roles like this exist because advanced AI systems are becoming large, complex, and socially important.   • Translate ambiguous research/product/systems goals into concrete technical work.
  • Build experiments, infrastructure, evaluations, dashboards, tools, or model pipelines.
  • Debug failures across models, data, infrastructure, and human workflows.
  • Communicate findings through technical writing, design docs, reports, or presentations.

Build larger projects with tests, documentation, monitoring, and clear design decisions. Contribute to open source or collaborate with researchers/builders. Publish technical writeups.

Own a domain deeply: interpretability, ML systems, GPU performance, AI safety, RL, product engineering, or data infrastructure. Show evidence that you can take ambiguous problems to concrete results.

This role is competitive. It is usually not an immediate beginner role unless it is explicitly an internship or fellowship. Linux + systems debugging

Distributed systems

Docker/Kubernetes
Run reproducible services and training jobs
Performance profiling
Find bottlenecks in code and infrastructure
Additional cross-cutting skills for this role: - Research writing: explain motivation, method, results, limitations, and next steps. - Experiment design: avoid misleading conclusions from noisy results. - Communication: make technical work understandable to teammates and stakeholders. - AI-assisted workflow: use AI to move faster, but verify everything important.

Proof of Work Projects

Project Level Time Expected Output
Model-serving API with load test Intermediate 3–6 weeks API + latency report
Distributed training toy demo Advanced 6–10 weeks Repo + writeup
Observability dashboard Intermediate 3–6 weeks Dashboard + metrics
A strong project for this role should include: 1. A clear problem statement. 2. A reproducible GitHub repository. 3. A short writeup explaining what you tried. 4. Results, screenshots, logs, benchmarks, or evaluation data. 5. A section on limitations and what you would improve next.

What AI Automation Replaces

  • Boilerplate code generation.
  • First drafts of documentation and reports.

  • Simple debugging suggestions.
  • Test case generation.
  • Plot/table generation.
  • Basic data cleaning or log summarization.
  • Scaffolding for APIs, dashboards, or experiments.

Why Humans Matter

  • Choosing the right problem is harder than generating code.
  • AI-generated experiments can be misleading without human judgment.
  • Safety, correctness, and reliability require accountability.
  • Real systems fail in messy ways that need context and ownership.
  • Research taste and product taste are difficult to automate fully.
  • Cross-team trust, prioritization, and communication remain human-heavy.
  • Someone must decide what evidence is strong enough to act on.

Research Manager, Interpretability

Prepare: 5–10+ years
Salary: $350k–$500k USD
Risk: Low

Supports teams reverse-engineering how large language models work internally at a mechanistic level. In practical terms, this role is about turning ambiguous AI problems into working systems, experiments, evaluations, tools, or research artifacts. It is not only about knowing AI concepts; it requires building, measuring, debugging, writing, and making decisions under uncertainty. For a student, the most important thing to understand is that this role rewards proof of ability: projects, research notes, reproducible experiments, open-source contributions, clear writing, and evidence that you can work through hard problems without needing perfect instructions.

Roles like this exist because advanced AI systems are becoming large, complex, and socially important. They need people who can improve capability while also thinking about reliability, safety, correctness, interpretability, performance, and real-world impact. AI can help with code and analysis, but high-stakes AI work still needs humans who can decide what to measure, which failures matter, and what should be trusted. By 2050, many routine tasks inside this role may be automated. The role may still remain valuable because the hard part is not only generating code or text; it is choosing good questions, designing robust systems, interpreting results, and taking responsibility for outcomes.

What you do

  • Translate ambiguous research/product/systems goals into concrete technical work.
  • Build experiments, infrastructure, evaluations, dashboards, tools, or model pipelines.
  • Debug failures across models, data, infrastructure, and human workflows.
  • Communicate findings through technical writing, design docs, reports, or presentations.
  • Collaborate with researchers, engineers, product teams, safety teams, or external experts.
  • Maintain high standards for reliability, reproducibility, and responsible deployment.
  • Use AI tools as accelerators while still verifying outputs and making final judgments.

Preparation Timeline

Year 1 — Core foundations
Learn Python deeply, Git/GitHub, Linux CLI, data structures, debugging, and basic ML concepts. Build small but clean projects and write about them.
Year 2 — Serious technical projects
Choose a direction and build projects that require reliability: experiments, dashboards, benchmarks, data pipelines, model-serving APIs, or systems tools. Start reading papers and technical blogs.
Years 3–4 — Production-style proof of work
Build larger projects with tests, documentation, monitoring, and clear design decisions. Contribute to open source or collaborate with researchers/builders. Publish technical writeups.
Years 4–7 — Specialization and ownership
Own a domain deeply: interpretability, ML systems, GPU performance, AI safety, RL, product engineering, or data infrastructure. Show evidence that you can take ambiguous problems to concrete results.
Years 7–10+ — Leadership or senior research track
For manager/lead roles, build a record of mentoring people, setting direction, recruiting, prioritizing projects, and communicating technical strategy. For senior IC roles, build a public record of high-impact systems or research contributions.

Reality Check

This role is competitive. It is usually not an immediate beginner role unless it is explicitly an internship or fellowship. A first-year student should treat it as a long-term target and build toward it through small public projects, then larger reproducible projects, then collaborations, internships, research-style writing, or production-quality systems. Do not only collect certificates. For this kind of role, visible proof matters more: GitHub repositories, demos, benchmarks, technical writeups, open-source contributions, and evidence that you can finish hard projects.

Core Skills

Python + PyTorch/JAX
Implement and inspect model internals
Transformer circuits
Understand attention heads, residual streams, features, superposition
Activation analysis
Extract and intervene on activations
Research tooling
Build tools researchers can use repeatedly
Additional cross-cutting skills for this role: - Research writing: explain motivation, method, results, limitations, and next steps. - Experiment design: avoid misleading conclusions from noisy results. - Communication: make technical work understandable to teammates and stakeholders. - AI-assisted workflow: use AI to move faster, but verify everything important.

Proof of Work Projects

Project Level Time Expected Output
Activation patching notebook Intermediate 3–6 weeks Notebook + explanation
Feature visualization mini-tool Intermediate 4–8 weeks Tool + case study
Transformer circuits reproduction Advanced 6–12 weeks Repo + writeup
A strong project for this role should include: 1. A clear problem statement. 2. A reproducible GitHub repository. 3. A short writeup explaining what you tried. 4. Results, screenshots, logs, benchmarks, or evaluation data. 5. A section on limitations and what you would improve next.

What AI Automation Replaces

  • Boilerplate code generation.
  • First drafts of documentation and reports.
  • Literature and documentation summaries.
  • Simple debugging suggestions.
  • Test case generation.
  • Plot/table generation.
  • Basic data cleaning or log summarization.
  • Scaffolding for APIs, dashboards, or experiments.

Why Humans Matter

  • Choosing the right problem is harder than generating code.
  • AI-generated experiments can be misleading without human judgment.
  • Safety, correctness, and reliability require accountability.
  • Real systems fail in messy ways that need context and ownership.
  • Research taste and product taste are difficult to automate fully.
  • Cross-team trust, prioritization, and communication remain human-heavy.
  • Someone must decide what evidence is strong enough to act on.

Research Engineer, Interpretability

Prepare: 3–7+ years
Salary: $315k–$560k USD
Risk: Low

Builds infrastructure and tools for activation extraction, steering, circuit tracing, and model internals research. In practical terms, this role is about turning ambiguous AI problems into working systems, experiments, evaluations, tools, or research artifacts. It is not only about knowing AI concepts; it requires building, measuring, debugging, writing, and making decisions under uncertainty. For a student, the most important thing to understand is that this role rewards proof of ability: projects, research notes, reproducible experiments, open-source contributions, clear writing, and evidence that you can work through hard problems without needing perfect instructions.

Roles like this exist because advanced AI systems are becoming large, complex, and socially important. They need people who can improve capability while also thinking about reliability, safety, correctness, interpretability, performance, and real-world impact. AI can help with code and analysis, but high-stakes AI work still needs humans who can decide what to measure, which failures matter, and what should be trusted. By 2050, many routine tasks inside this role may be automated. The role may still remain valuable because the hard part is not only generating code or text; it is choosing good questions, designing robust systems, interpreting results, and taking responsibility for outcomes.

What you do

  • Translate ambiguous research/product/systems goals into concrete technical work.
  • Build experiments, infrastructure, evaluations, dashboards, tools, or model pipelines.
  • Debug failures across models, data, infrastructure, and human workflows.
  • Communicate findings through technical writing, design docs, reports, or presentations.
  • Collaborate with researchers, engineers, product teams, safety teams, or external experts.
  • Maintain high standards for reliability, reproducibility, and responsible deployment.
  • Use AI tools as accelerators while still verifying outputs and making final judgments.

Preparation Timeline

Month 0–3 — Foundations
Learn Python, Git/GitHub, Linux CLI, basic math, and how to read technical documentation. Build 2–3 small public repositories.
Month 3–6 — Applied ML and systems basics
Learn ML fundamentals, model evaluation, APIs, data handling, and basic backend or notebook workflows. Build one reproducible ML/evaluation project.
Month 6–12 — Role-specific projects
Pick one subfield and build a focused project: benchmark, dashboard, model-serving app, RL environment, safety classifier, interpretability notebook, or data pipeline.
Month 12–24 — Public proof of ability
Reproduce a small paper/blog result, publish a technical writeup, improve the project quality, and create a portfolio page with clear motivation, method, results, and limitations.
Beyond 24 months — Specialization
Deepen one track through internships, open-source work, research collaborations, production projects, or advanced systems/research projects.

Reality Check

This role is competitive. It is usually not an immediate beginner role unless it is explicitly an internship or fellowship. A first-year student should treat it as a long-term target and build toward it through small public projects, then larger reproducible projects, then collaborations, internships, research-style writing, or production-quality systems. Do not only collect certificates. For this kind of role, visible proof matters more: GitHub repositories, demos, benchmarks, technical writeups, open-source contributions, and evidence that you can finish hard projects.

Core Skills

Python + PyTorch/JAX
Implement and inspect model internals
Transformer circuits
Understand attention heads, residual streams, features, superposition
Activation analysis
Extract and intervene on activations
Research tooling
Build tools researchers can use repeatedly
Additional cross-cutting skills for this role: - Research writing: explain motivation, method, results, limitations, and next steps. - Experiment design: avoid misleading conclusions from noisy results. - Communication: make technical work understandable to teammates and stakeholders. - AI-assisted workflow: use AI to move faster, but verify everything important.

Proof of Work Projects

Project Level Time Expected Output
Activation patching notebook Intermediate 3–6 weeks Notebook + explanation
Feature visualization mini-tool Intermediate 4–8 weeks Tool + case study
Transformer circuits reproduction Advanced 6–12 weeks Repo + writeup
A strong project for this role should include: 1. A clear problem statement. 2. A reproducible GitHub repository. 3. A short writeup explaining what you tried. 4. Results, screenshots, logs, benchmarks, or evaluation data. 5. A section on limitations and what you would improve next.

What AI Automation Replaces

  • Boilerplate code generation.
  • First drafts of documentation and reports.
  • Literature and documentation summaries.
  • Simple debugging suggestions.
  • Test case generation.
  • Plot/table generation.
  • Basic data cleaning or log summarization.
  • Scaffolding for APIs, dashboards, or experiments.

Why Humans Matter

  • Choosing the right problem is harder than generating code.
  • AI-generated experiments can be misleading without human judgment.
  • Safety, correctness, and reliability require accountability.
  • Real systems fail in messy ways that need context and ownership.
  • Research taste and product taste are difficult to automate fully.
  • Cross-team trust, prioritization, and communication remain human-heavy.
  • Someone must decide what evidence is strong enough to act on.

Research Engineer / Research Scientist, Alignment Science

Prepare: 3–7+ years
Salary: $350k–$500k USD / £260k–£370k GBP
Risk: Low–Medium

Empirical AI safety: scalable oversight, AI control, stress testing, model welfare, safety evaluations. In practical terms, this role is about turning ambiguous AI problems into working systems, experiments, evaluations, tools, or research artifacts. It is not only about knowing AI concepts; it requires building, measuring, debugging, writing, and making decisions under uncertainty. For a student, the most important thing to understand is that this role rewards proof of ability: projects, research notes, reproducible experiments, open-source contributions, clear writing, and evidence that you can work through hard problems without needing perfect instructions.

Roles like this exist because advanced AI systems are becoming large, complex, and socially important. They need people who can improve capability while also thinking about reliability, safety, correctness, interpretability, performance, and real-world impact. AI can help with code and analysis, but high-stakes AI work still needs humans who can decide what to measure, which failures matter, and what should be trusted. By 2050, many routine tasks inside this role may be automated. The role may still remain valuable because the hard part is not only generating code or text; it is choosing good questions, designing robust systems, interpreting results, and taking responsibility for outcomes.

  • Translate ambiguous research/product/systems goals into concrete technical work.
  • Build experiments, infrastructure, evaluations, dashboards, tools, or model pipelines.
  • Debug failures across models, data, infrastructure, and human workflows.
  • Communicate findings through technical writing, design docs, reports, or presentations.
  • Collaborate with researchers, engineers, product teams, safety teams, or external experts.
  • Maintain high standards for reliability, reproducibility, and responsible deployment.
  • Use AI tools as accelerators while still verifying outputs and making final judgments.

Preparation Timeline

Month 0–3 — Foundations
Learn Python, Git/GitHub, Linux CLI, basic math, and how to read technical documentation. Build 2–3 small public repositories.
Month 3–6 — Applied ML and systems basics
Learn ML fundamentals, model evaluation, APIs, data handling, and basic backend or notebook workflows. Build one reproducible ML/evaluation project.
Month 6–12 — Role-specific projects
Pick one subfield and build a focused project: benchmark, dashboard, model-serving app, RL environment, safety classifier, interpretability notebook, or data pipeline.
Month 12–24 — Public proof of ability
Reproduce a small paper/blog result, publish a technical writeup, improve the project quality, and create a portfolio page with clear motivation, method, results, and limitations.
Beyond 24 months — Specialization
Deepen one track through internships, open-source work, research collaborations, production projects, or advanced systems/research projects.

Reality Check

This role is competitive. It is usually not an immediate beginner role unless it is explicitly an internship or fellowship. A first-year student should treat it as a long-term target and build toward it through small public projects, then larger reproducible projects, then collaborations, internships, research-style writing, or production-quality systems. Do not only collect certificates. For this kind of role, visible proof matters more: GitHub repositories, demos, benchmarks, technical writeups, open-source contributions, and evidence that you can finish hard projects.

Core Skills

Python
Build evaluations and analysis pipelines
LLM evaluation
Measure honesty, safety, and model behavior
Statistics
Interpret noisy evaluation results
AI safety basics
Understand alignment, control, oversight, and failure modes
Additional cross-cutting skills for this role: - Research writing: explain motivation, method, results, limitations, and next steps. - Experiment design: avoid misleading conclusions from noisy results. - Communication: make technical work understandable to teammates and stakeholders. - AI-assisted workflow: use AI to move faster, but verify everything important.

Proof of Work Projects

Project Level Time Expected Output
LLM honesty benchmark Beginner–Intermediate 1–3 weeks Dataset + report
Prompt injection test suite Intermediate 2–4 weeks Tests + findings
Safety classifier prototype Intermediate 4–8 weeks Classifier + eval report
A strong project for this role should include: 1. A clear problem statement. 2. A reproducible GitHub repository. 3. A short writeup explaining what you tried. 4. Results, screenshots, logs, benchmarks, or evaluation data. 5. A section on limitations and what you would improve next.

What AI Automation Replaces

  • Boilerplate code generation.
  • First drafts of documentation and reports.
  • Literature and documentation summaries.
  • Simple debugging suggestions.
  • Test case generation.
  • Plot/table generation.
  • Basic data cleaning or log summarization.
  • Scaffolding for APIs, dashboards, or experiments.

Why Humans Matter

  • Choosing the right problem is harder than generating code.
  • AI-generated experiments can be misleading without human judgment.
  • Safety, correctness, and reliability require accountability.
  • Real systems fail in messy ways that need context and ownership.
  • Research taste and product taste are difficult to automate fully.
  • Cross-team trust, prioritization, and communication remain human-heavy.
  • Someone must decide what evidence is strong enough to act on.

Best Next Step

Pick one small project from the table above. Build it in public. Write a clear README with: problem, method, results, limitations, and next steps. Then improve it once based on your own critique.

Official Source Links

  • Official Anthropic job board
  • Anthropic careers
  • Candidate AI usage guidance
  • Alignment Science blog
  • Frontier Red Team blog
  • Transformer Circuits

Research Scientist / Engineer, Honesty

Prepare: 3–7+ years
Salary: $350k–$500k USD
Risk: Low–Medium

Reduces hallucinations and improves truthfulness, calibration, confidence, and factual grounding in language models. In practical terms, this role is about turning ambiguous AI problems into working systems, experiments, evaluations, tools, or research artifacts. It is not only about knowing AI concepts; it requires building, measuring, debugging, writing, and making decisions under uncertainty. For a student, the most important thing to understand is that this role rewards proof of ability: projects, research notes, reproducible experiments, open-source contributions, clear writing, and evidence that you can work through hard problems without needing perfect instructions.

Roles like this exist because advanced AI systems are becoming large, complex, and socially important. They need people who can improve capability while also thinking about reliability, safety, correctness, interpretability, performance, and real-world impact. AI can help with code and analysis, but high-stakes AI work still needs humans who can decide what to measure, which failures matter, and what should be trusted. By 2050, many routine tasks inside this role may be automated. The role may still remain valuable because the hard part is not only generating code or text; it is choosing good questions, designing robust systems, interpreting results, and taking responsibility for outcomes.

What you do

  • Translate ambiguous research/product/systems goals into concrete technical work.
  • Build experiments, infrastructure, evaluations, dashboards, tools, or model pipelines.
  • Debug failures across models, data, infrastructure, and human workflows.
  • Communicate findings through technical writing, design docs, reports, or presentations.
  • Collaborate with researchers, engineers, product teams, safety teams, or external experts.
  • Maintain high standards for reliability, reproducibility, and responsible deployment.
  • Use AI tools as accelerators while still verifying outputs and making final judgments.

Preparation Timeline

Month 0–3 — Foundations
Learn Python, Git/GitHub, Linux CLI, basic math, and how to read technical documentation. Build 2–3 small public repositories.
Month 3–6 — Applied ML and systems basics
Learn ML fundamentals, model evaluation, APIs, data handling, and basic backend or notebook workflows. Build one reproducible ML/evaluation project.
Month 6–12 — Role-specific projects
Pick one subfield and build a focused project: benchmark, dashboard, model-serving app, RL environment, safety classifier, interpretability notebook, or data pipeline.
Month 12–24 — Public proof of ability
Reproduce a small paper/blog result, publish a technical writeup, improve the project quality, and create a portfolio page with clear motivation, method, results, and limitations.
Beyond 24 months — Specialization
Deepen one track through internships, open-source work, research collaborations, production projects, or advanced systems/research projects.

Reality Check

This role is competitive. It is usually not an immediate beginner role unless it is explicitly an internship or fellowship. A first-year student should treat it as a long-term target and build toward it through small public projects, then larger reproducible projects, then collaborations, internships, research-style writing, or production-quality systems. Do not only collect certificates. For this kind of role, visible proof matters more: GitHub repositories, demos, benchmarks, technical writeups, open-source contributions, and evidence that you can finish hard projects.

Core Skills

Python
Build evaluations and analysis pipelines
LLM evaluation
Measure honesty, safety, and model behavior
Statistics
Interpret noisy evaluation results
AI safety basics
Understand alignment, control, oversight, and failure modes
Additional cross-cutting skills for this role: - Research writing: explain motivation, method, results, limitations, and next steps. - Experiment design: avoid misleading conclusions from noisy results. - Communication: make technical work understandable to teammates and stakeholders. - AI-assisted workflow: use AI to move faster, but verify everything important.

Proof of Work Projects

Project Level Time Expected Output
LLM honesty benchmark Beginner–Intermediate 1–3 weeks Dataset + report
Prompt injection test suite Intermediate 2–4 weeks Tests + findings
Safety classifier prototype Intermediate 4–8 weeks Classifier + eval report
A strong project for this role should include: 1. A clear problem statement. 2. A reproducible GitHub repository. 3. A short writeup explaining what you tried. 4. Results, screenshots, logs, benchmarks, or evaluation data. 5. A section on limitations and what you would improve next.

What AI Automation Replaces

  • Boilerplate code generation.
  • First drafts of documentation and reports.
  • Literature and documentation summaries.
  • Simple debugging suggestions.
  • Test case generation.
  • Plot/table generation.
  • Basic data cleaning or log summarization.
  • Scaffolding for APIs, dashboards, or experiments.

Why Humans Matter

  • Choosing the right problem is harder than generating code.
  • AI-generated experiments can be misleading without human judgment.
  • Safety, correctness, and reliability require accountability.
  • Real systems fail in messy ways that need context and ownership.
  • Research taste and product taste are difficult to automate fully.
  • Cross-team trust, prioritization, and communication remain human-heavy.
  • Someone must decide what evidence is strong enough to act on.

Best Next Step

Pick one small project from the table above. Build it in public. Write a clear README with: problem, method, results, limitations, and next steps. Then improve it once based on your own critique.

  • Debug failures across models, data, infrastructure, and human workflows.
  • Communicate findings through technical writing, design docs, reports, or presentations.
  • Collaborate with researchers, engineers, product teams, safety teams, or external experts.
  • Maintain high standards for reliability, reproducibility, and responsible deployment.
  • Use AI tools as accelerators while still verifying outputs and making final judgments.

Preparation Timeline

Month 0–3 — Foundations
Learn Python, Git/GitHub, Linux CLI, basic math, and how to read technical documentation. Build 2–3 small public repositories.
Month 3–6 — Applied ML and systems basics
Learn ML fundamentals, model evaluation, APIs, data handling, and basic backend or notebook workflows. Build one reproducible ML/evaluation project.
Month 6–12 — Role-specific projects
Pick one subfield and build a focused project: benchmark, dashboard, model-serving app, RL environment, safety classifier, interpretability notebook, or data pipeline.
Month 12–24 — Public proof of ability
Reproduce a small paper/blog result, publish a technical writeup, improve the project quality, and create a portfolio page with clear motivation, method, results, and limitations.
Beyond 24 months — Specialization
Deepen one track through internships, open-source work, research collaborations, production projects, or advanced systems/research projects.

Reality Check

This role is competitive. It is usually not an immediate beginner role unless it is explicitly an internship or fellowship. A first-year student should treat it as a long-term target and build toward it through small public projects, then larger reproducible projects, then collaborations, internships, research-style writing, or production-quality systems. Do not only collect certificates. For this kind of role, visible proof matters more: GitHub repositories, demos, benchmarks, technical writeups, open-source contributions, and evidence that you can finish hard projects.

Core Skills

Python async/concurrency
Build fast research pipelines and environments
RL fundamentals
Understand rewards, policies, environments, and training loops
PyTorch/JAX
Implement and debug model training
Evaluation design
Measure agent behavior reliably
Additional cross-cutting skills for this role: - Research writing: explain motivation, method, results, limitations, and next steps. - Experiment design: avoid misleading conclusions from noisy results. - Communication: make technical work understandable to teammates and stakeholders. - AI-assisted workflow: use AI to move faster, but verify everything important.

Proof of Work Projects

Project Level Time Expected Output
Gridworld RL environment Beginner–Intermediate 1–2 weeks Environment + notes
Code-verifier benchmark Intermediate 3–6 weeks Verifier + tasks
Long-horizon agent sandbox Advanced 6–12 weeks Sandbox + report
A strong project for this role should include: 1. A clear problem statement. 2. A reproducible GitHub repository. 3. A short writeup explaining what you tried. 4. Results, screenshots, logs, benchmarks, or evaluation data. 5. A section on limitations and what you would improve next.

What AI Automation Replaces

  • Boilerplate code generation.
  • First drafts of documentation and reports.
  • Literature and documentation summaries.
  • Simple debugging suggestions.
  • Test case generation.
  • Plot/table generation.
  • Basic data cleaning or log summarization.
  • Scaffolding for APIs, dashboards, or experiments.

Why Humans Matter

  • Choosing the right problem is harder than generating code.
  • AI-generated experiments can be misleading without human judgment.
  • Safety, correctness, and reliability require accountability.
  • Real systems fail in messy ways that need context and ownership.
  • Research taste and product taste are difficult to automate fully.
  • Cross-team trust, prioritization, and communication remain human-heavy.
  • Someone must decide what evidence is strong enough to act on.

Best Next Step

Pick one small project from the table above. Build it in public. Write a clear README with: problem, method, results, limitations, and next steps. Then improve it once based on your own critique.

  • Official Anthropic job board
  • Anthropic careers
  • Candidate AI usage guidance
  • Alignment Science blog
  • Frontier Red Team blog
  • Transformer Circuits
