Research Engineer, Code RL

Prepare: 3–7+ years
Salary: $500k–$850k USD
Risk: Low

Trains models to write, edit, test, debug, and ship real software using RL environments, verifiers, and coding task pipelines. In practical terms, this role is about turning ambiguous AI problems into working systems, experiments, evaluations, tools, or research artifacts. It is not only about knowing AI concepts; it requires building, measuring, debugging, writing, and making decisions under uncertainty. For a student, the most important thing to understand is that this role rewards proof of ability: projects, research notes, reproducible experiments, open-source contributions, clear writing, and evidence that you can work through hard problems without needing perfect instructions.

Roles like this exist because advanced AI systems are becoming large, complex, and socially important. They need people who can improve capability while also thinking about reliability, safety, correctness, interpretability, performance, and real-world impact. AI can help with code and analysis, but high-stakes AI work still needs humans who can decide what to measure, which failures matter, and what should be trusted. By 2050, many routine tasks inside this role may be automated. The role may still remain valuable because the hard part is not only generating code or text; it is choosing good questions, designing robust systems, interpreting results, and taking responsibility for outcomes.

What you do

  • Translate ambiguous research/product/systems goals into concrete technical work.
  • Build experiments, infrastructure, evaluations, dashboards, tools, or model pipelines.
  • Debug failures across models, data, infrastructure, and human workflows.
  • Communicate findings through technical writing, design docs, reports, or presentations.
  • Collaborate with researchers, engineers, product teams, safety teams, or external experts.
  • Maintain high standards for reliability, reproducibility, and responsible deployment.
  • Use AI tools as accelerators while still verifying outputs and making final judgments.

Preparation Timeline

Month 0–3 — Foundations
Learn Python, Git/GitHub, Linux CLI, basic math, and how to read technical documentation. Build 2–3 small public repositories.
Month 3–6 — Applied ML and systems basics
Learn ML fundamentals, model evaluation, APIs, data handling, and basic backend or notebook workflows. Build one reproducible ML/evaluation project.
Month 6–12 — Role-specific projects
Pick one subfield and build a focused project: benchmark, dashboard, model-serving app, RL environment, safety classifier, interpretability notebook, or data pipeline.
Month 12–24 — Public proof of ability
Reproduce a small paper/blog result, publish a technical writeup, improve the project quality, and create a portfolio page with clear motivation, method, results, and limitations.
Beyond 24 months — Specialization
Deepen one track through internships, open-source work, research collaborations, production projects, or advanced systems/research projects.

Reality Check

This role is competitive. It is usually not an immediate beginner role unless it is explicitly an internship or fellowship. A first-year student should treat it as a long-term target and build toward it through small public projects, then larger reproducible projects, then collaborations, internships, research-style writing, or production-quality systems. Do not only collect certificates. For this kind of role, visible proof matters more: GitHub repositories, demos, benchmarks, technical writeups, open-source contributions, and evidence that you can finish hard projects.

Core Skills

Python async/concurrency
Build fast research pipelines and environments
RL fundamentals
Understand rewards, policies, environments, and training loops
PyTorch/JAX
Implement and debug model training
Evaluation design
Measure agent behavior reliably
Additional cross-cutting skills for this role: - Research writing: explain motivation, method, results, limitations, and next steps. - Experiment design: avoid misleading conclusions from noisy results. - Communication: make technical work understandable to teammates and stakeholders. - AI-assisted workflow: use AI to move faster, but verify everything important.

Proof of Work Projects

Project Level Time Expected Output
Gridworld RL environment Beginner–Intermediate 1–2 weeks Environment + notes
Code-verifier benchmark Intermediate 3–6 weeks Verifier + tasks
Long-horizon agent sandbox Advanced 6–12 weeks Sandbox + report
A strong project for this role should include: 1. A clear problem statement. 2. A reproducible GitHub repository. 3. A short writeup explaining what you tried. 4. Results, screenshots, logs, benchmarks, or evaluation data. 5. A section on limitations and what you would improve next.

What AI Automation Replaces

  • Boilerplate code generation.
  • First drafts of documentation and reports.
  • Literature and documentation summaries.
  • Simple debugging suggestions.
  • Test case generation.
  • Plot/table generation.
  • Basic data cleaning or log summarization.
  • Scaffolding for APIs, dashboards, or experiments.

Why Humans Matter

  • Choosing the right problem is harder than generating code.
  • AI-generated experiments can be misleading without human judgment.
  • Safety, correctness, and reliability require accountability.
  • Real systems fail in messy ways that need context and ownership.
  • Research taste and product taste are difficult to automate fully.
  • Cross-team trust, prioritization, and communication remain human-heavy.
  • Someone must decide what evidence is strong enough to act on.

Best Next Step

Pick one small project from the table above. Build it in public. Write a clear README with: problem, method, results, limitations, and next steps. Then improve it once based on your own critique.

Research Engineer, Machine Learning (RL Velocity)

Prepare: 3–7+ years

Research Engineer, Performance RL

Prepare: 4–8+ years
Salary: $350k–$850k USD
Risk: Low

Teaches models to write correct, fast code for accelerators using RL environments, evaluations, and accelerator performance expertise. In practical terms, this role is about turning ambiguous AI problems into working systems, experiments, evaluations, tools, or research artifacts. It is not only about knowing AI concepts; it requires building, measuring, debugging, writing, and making decisions under uncertainty. For a student, the most important thing to understand is that this role rewards proof of ability: projects, research notes, reproducible experiments, open-source contributions, clear writing, and evidence that you can work through hard problems without needing perfect instructions.

Roles like this exist because advanced AI systems are becoming large, complex, and socially important. They need people who can improve capability while also thinking about reliability, safety, correctness, interpretability, performance, and real-world impact. AI can help with code and analysis, but high-stakes AI work still needs humans who can decide what to measure, which failures matter, and what should be trusted. By 2050, many routine tasks inside this role may be automated. The role may still remain valuable because the hard part is not only generating code or text; it is choosing good questions, designing robust systems, interpreting results, and taking responsibility for outcomes.

What you do

  • Translate ambiguous research/product/systems goals into concrete technical work.
  • Build experiments, infrastructure, evaluations, dashboards, tools, or model pipelines.
  • Debug failures across models, data, infrastructure, and human workflows.
  • Communicate findings through technical writing, design docs, reports, or presentations.
  • Collaborate with researchers, engineers, product teams, safety teams, or external experts.
  • Maintain high standards for reliability, reproducibility, and responsible deployment.
  • Use AI tools as accelerators while still verifying outputs and making final judgments.

Preparation Timeline

Month 0–3 — Foundations
Learn Python, Git/GitHub, Linux CLI, basic math, and how to read technical documentation. Build 2–3 small public repositories.
Month 3–6 — Applied ML and systems basics
Learn ML fundamentals, model evaluation, APIs, data handling, and basic backend or notebook workflows. Build one reproducible ML/evaluation project.
Month 6–12 — Role-specific projects
Pick one subfield and build a focused project: benchmark, dashboard, model-serving app, RL environment, safety classifier, interpretability notebook, or data pipeline.
Month 12–24 — Public proof of ability
Reproduce a small paper/blog result, publish a technical writeup, improve the project quality, and create a portfolio page with clear motivation, method, results, and limitations.
Beyond 24 months — Specialization
Deepen one track through internships, open-source work, research collaborations, production projects, or advanced systems/research projects.

Reality Check

This role is competitive. It is usually not an immediate beginner role unless it is explicitly an internship or fellowship. A first-year student should treat it as a long-term target and build toward it through small public projects, then larger reproducible projects, then collaborations, internships, research-style writing, or production-quality systems. Do not only collect certificates. For this kind of role, visible proof matters more: GitHub repositories, demos, benchmarks, technical writeups, open-source contributions, and evidence that you can finish hard projects.

Core Skills

CUDA/Triton basics
Write and optimize kernels
GPU architecture
Understand memory bandwidth, tensor cores, and occupancy
Profiling tools
Use Nsight/profilers to locate bottlenecks
Low precision inference
Understand FP8/INT8, quantization, and numerical tradeoffs
Additional cross-cutting skills for this role: - Research writing: explain motivation, method, results, limitations, and next steps. - Experiment design: avoid misleading conclusions from noisy results. - Communication: make technical work understandable to teammates and stakeholders. - AI-assisted workflow: use AI to move faster, but verify everything important.

Proof of Work Projects

Project Level Time Expected Output
CUDA matrix multiplication Intermediate 3–6 weeks Kernel + benchmark
Triton attention microbenchmark Advanced 4–8 weeks Benchmark + report
Quantized inference experiment Advanced 4–8 weeks Accuracy/latency analysis
A strong project for this role should include: 1. A clear problem statement. 2. A reproducible GitHub repository. 3. A short writeup explaining what you tried. 4. Results, screenshots, logs, benchmarks, or evaluation data. 5. A section on limitations and what you would improve next.

What AI Automation Replaces

  • Boilerplate code generation.
  • First drafts of documentation and reports.
  • Literature and documentation summaries.
  • Simple debugging suggestions.
  • Test case generation.
  • Plot/table generation.
  • Basic data cleaning or log summarization.
  • Scaffolding for APIs, dashboards, or experiments.

Why Humans Matter

  • Choosing the right problem is harder than generating code.
  • AI-generated experiments can be misleading without human judgment.
  • Safety, correctness, and reliability require accountability.
  • Real systems fail in messy ways that need context and ownership.
  • Research taste and product taste are difficult to automate fully.
  • Cross-team trust, prioritization, and communication remain human-heavy.
  • Someone must decide what evidence is strong enough to act on.

Builds and improves RL algorithms and infrastructure used by researchers to train models with RLHF and related methods. In practical terms, this role is about turning ambiguous AI problems into working systems, experiments, evaluations, tools, or research artifacts. It is not only about knowing AI concepts; it requires building, measuring, debugging, writing, and making decisions under uncertainty. Roles like this exist because advanced AI systems are becoming large, complex, and socially important. They need people who can improve capability while also thinking about reliability, safety, correctness, interpretability, performance, and real-world impact. AI can help with code and analysis, but high-stakes AI work still needs humans who can decide what to measure, which failures matter, and what should be trusted. By 2050, many routine tasks inside this role may be automated. The role may still remain valuable because the hard part is not only generating code or text; it is choosing good questions, designing robust systems, interpreting results, and taking responsibility for outcomes.

What you do

  • Translate ambiguous research/product/systems goals into concrete technical work.
  • Build experiments, infrastructure, evaluations, dashboards, tools, or model pipelines.
  • Debug failures across models, data, infrastructure, and human workflows.
  • Communicate findings through technical writing, design docs, reports, or presentations.
  • Collaborate with researchers, engineers, product teams, safety teams, or external experts.
  • Maintain high standards for reliability, reproducibility, and responsible deployment.
  • Use AI tools as accelerators while still verifying outputs and making final judgments.

Preparation Timeline

Month 0–3 — Foundations
Learn Python, Git/GitHub, Linux CLI, basic math, and how to read technical documentation. Build 2–3 small public repositories.
Month 3–6 — Applied ML and systems basics
Learn ML fundamentals, model evaluation, APIs, data handling, and basic backend or notebook workflows. Build one reproducible ML/evaluation project.
Month 6–12 — Role-specific projects
Pick one subfield and build a focused project: benchmark, dashboard, model-serving app, RL environment, safety classifier, interpretability notebook, or data pipeline.
Month 12–24 — Public proof of ability
Reproduce a small paper/blog result, publish a technical writeup, improve the project quality, and create a portfolio page with clear motivation, method, results, and limitations.
Beyond 24 months — Specialization
Deepen one track through internships, open-source work, research collaborations, production projects, or advanced systems/research projects.

Reality Check

This role is competitive. It is usually not an immediate beginner role unless it is explicitly an internship or fellowship. A first-year student should treat it as a long-term target and build toward it through small public projects, then larger reproducible projects, then collaborations, internships, research-style writing, or production-quality systems. Do not only collect certificates. For this kind of role, visible proof matters more: GitHub repositories, demos, benchmarks, technical writeups, open-source contributions, and evidence that you can finish hard projects.

Core Skills

Python async/concurrency
Build fast research pipelines and environments
RL fundamentals
Understand rewards, policies, environments, and training loops
PyTorch/JAX
Implement and debug model training
Evaluation design
Measure agent behavior reliably
Additional cross-cutting skills for this role: - Research writing: explain motivation, method, results, limitations, and next steps. - Experiment design: avoid misleading conclusions from noisy results. - Communication: make technical work understandable to teammates and stakeholders. - AI-assisted workflow: use AI to move faster, but verify everything important.

Proof of Work Projects

Project Level Time Expected Output
Gridworld RL environment Beginner–Intermediate 1–2 weeks Environment + notes
Code-verifier benchmark Intermediate 3–6 weeks Verifier + tasks
Long-horizon agent sandbox Advanced 6–12 weeks Sandbox + report
A strong project for this role should include: 1. A clear problem statement. 2. A reproducible GitHub repository. 3. A short writeup explaining what you tried. 4. Results, screenshots, logs, benchmarks, or evaluation data. 5. A section on limitations and what you would improve next.

What AI Automation Replaces

  • Boilerplate code generation.
  • First drafts of documentation and reports.
  • Literature and documentation summaries.
  • Simple debugging suggestions.
  • Test case generation.
  • Plot/table generation.
  • Basic data cleaning or log summarization.
  • Scaffolding for APIs, dashboards, or experiments.

Why Humans Matter

  • Choosing the right problem is harder than generating code.
  • AI-generated experiments can be misleading without human judgment.
  • Safety, correctness, and reliability require accountability.
  • Real systems fail in messy ways that need context and ownership.
  • Research taste and product taste are difficult to automate fully.
  • Cross-team trust, prioritization, and communication remain human-heavy.
  • Someone must decide what evidence is strong enough to act on.

Best Next Step

Pick one small project from the table above. Build it in public. Write a clear README with: problem, method, results, limitations, and next steps. Then improve it once based on your own critique.

Official Source Links

  • Official Anthropic job board
  • Anthropic careers
  • Candidate AI usage guidance
  • Alignment Science blog
  • Frontier Red Team blog
  • Transformer Circuits
Back to Directory

Research Engineer, Production Model Post-Training

Prepare: 3–7+ years
Salary: $350k–$500k USD
Risk: Low

Implements and scales post-training techniques such as Constitutional AI, RLHF, fine-tuning, evaluation, and model quality improvement. In practical terms, this role is about turning ambiguous AI problems into working systems, experiments, evaluations, tools, or research artifacts. It is not only about knowing AI concepts; it requires building, measuring, debugging, writing, and making decisions under uncertainty. For a student, the most important thing to understand is that this role rewards proof of ability: projects, research notes, reproducible experiments, open-source contributions, clear writing, and evidence that you can work through hard problems without needing perfect instructions.

Roles like this exist because advanced AI systems are becoming large, complex, and socially important. They need people who can improve capability while also thinking about reliability, safety, correctness, interpretability, performance, and real-world impact. AI can help with code and analysis, but high-stakes AI work still needs humans who can decide what to measure, which failures matter, and what should be trusted. By 2050, many routine tasks inside this role may be automated. The role may still remain valuable because the hard part is not only generating code or text; it is choosing good questions, designing robust systems, interpreting results, and taking responsibility for outcomes.

  • Translate ambiguous research/product/systems goals into concrete technical work.
  • Build experiments, infrastructure, evaluations, dashboards, tools, or model pipelines.
  • Debug failures across models, data, infrastructure, and human workflows.
  • Communicate findings through technical writing, design docs, reports, or presentations.
  • Collaborate with researchers, engineers, product teams, safety teams, or external experts.
  • Maintain high standards for reliability, reproducibility, and responsible deployment.
  • Use AI tools as accelerators while still verifying outputs and making final judgments.

Preparation Timeline

Month 6–12 — Role-specific projects
Pick one subfield and build a focused project: benchmark, dashboard, model-serving app, RL environment, safety classifier, interpretability notebook, or data pipeline.
Month 12–24 — Public proof of ability
Reproduce a small paper/blog result, publish a technical writeup, improve the project quality, and create a portfolio page with clear motivation, method, results, and limitations.
Beyond 24 months — Specialization
Deepen one track through internships, open-source work, research collaborations, production projects, or advanced systems/research projects.

Reality Check

This role is competitive. It is usually not an immediate beginner role unless it is explicitly an internship or fellowship. A first-year student should treat it as a long-term target and build toward it through small public projects, then larger reproducible projects, then collaborations, internships, research-style writing, or production-quality systems. Do not only collect certificates. For this kind of role, visible proof matters more: GitHub repositories, demos, benchmarks, technical writeups, open-source contributions, and evidence that you can finish hard projects.

Core Skills

Python async/concurrency
Build fast research pipelines and environments
RL fundamentals
Understand rewards, policies, environments, and training loops
PyTorch/JAX
Implement and debug model training
Evaluation design
Measure agent behavior reliably
Additional cross-cutting skills for this role: - Research writing: explain motivation, method, results, limitations, and next steps. - Experiment design: avoid misleading conclusions from noisy results. - Communication: make technical work understandable to teammates and stakeholders. - AI-assisted workflow: use AI to move faster, but verify everything important.

Proof of Work Projects

Project Level Time Expected Output
Gridworld RL environment Beginner–Intermediate 1–2 weeks Environment + notes
Code-verifier benchmark Intermediate 3–6 weeks Verifier + tasks
Long-horizon agent sandbox Advanced 6–12 weeks Sandbox + report
A strong project for this role should include: 1. A clear problem statement. 2. A reproducible GitHub repository. 3. A short writeup explaining what you tried. 4. Results, screenshots, logs, benchmarks, or evaluation data. 5. A section on limitations and what you would improve next.

What AI Automation Replaces

  • Boilerplate code generation.
  • First drafts of documentation and reports.
  • Literature and documentation summaries.
  • Simple debugging suggestions.
  • Test case generation.
  • Plot/table generation.
  • Basic data cleaning or log summarization.
  • Scaffolding for APIs, dashboards, or experiments.

Why Humans Matter

  • Choosing the right problem is harder than generating code.
  • AI-generated experiments can be misleading without human judgment.
  • Safety, correctness, and reliability require accountability.
  • Real systems fail in messy ways that need context and ownership.
  • Research taste and product taste are difficult to automate fully.
  • Cross-team trust, prioritization, and communication remain human-heavy.
  • Someone must decide what evidence is strong enough to act on.

Pick one small project from the table above. Build it in public. Write a clear README with: problem, method, results, limitations, and next steps. Then improve it once based on your own critique.

In practical terms, this role is about turning ambiguous AI problems into working systems, experiments, evaluations, tools, or research artifacts. It is not only about knowing AI concepts; it requires building, measuring, debugging, writing, and making decisions under uncertainty. For a student, the most important thing to understand is that this role rewards proof of ability: projects, research notes, reproducible experiments, open-source contributions, clear writing, and evidence that you can work through hard problems without needing perfect instructions.

Roles like this exist because advanced AI systems are becoming large, complex, and socially important. They need people who can improve capability while also thinking about reliability, safety, correctness, interpretability, performance, and real-world impact. AI can help with code and analysis, but high-stakes AI work still needs humans who can decide what to measure, which failures matter, and what should be trusted. By 2050, many routine tasks inside this role may be automated. The role may still remain valuable because the hard part is not only generating code or text; it is choosing good questions, designing robust systems, interpreting results, and taking responsibility for outcomes.

What you do

  • Translate ambiguous research/product/systems goals into concrete technical work.
  • Build experiments, infrastructure, evaluations, dashboards, tools, or model pipelines.
  • Debug failures across models, data, infrastructure, and human workflows.
  • Communicate findings through technical writing, design docs, reports, or presentations.
  • Collaborate with researchers, engineers, product teams, safety teams, or external experts.
  • Maintain high standards for reliability, reproducibility, and responsible deployment.

Preparation Timeline

Month 0–3 — Foundations
Learn Python, Git/GitHub, Linux CLI, basic math, and how to read technical documentation. Build 2–3 small public repositories.

Learn ML fundamentals, model evaluation, APIs, data handling, and basic backend or notebook workflows. Build one reproducible ML/evaluation project.
Month 6–12 — Role-specific projects
Pick one subfield and build a focused project: benchmark, dashboard, model-serving app, RL environment, safety classifier, interpretability notebook, or data pipeline.
Month 12–24 — Public proof of ability
Reproduce a small paper/blog result, publish a technical writeup, improve the project quality, and create a portfolio page with clear motivation, method, results, and limitations.
Beyond 24 months — Specialization
Deepen one track through internships, open-source work, research collaborations, production projects, or advanced systems/research projects.

Reality Check

This role is competitive. It is usually not an immediate beginner role unless it is explicitly an internship or fellowship. A first-year student should treat it as a long-term target and build toward it through small public projects, then larger reproducible projects, then collaborations, internships, research-style writing, or production-quality systems. Do not only collect certificates. For this kind of role, visible proof matters more: GitHub repositories, demos, benchmarks, technical writeups, open-source contributions, and evidence that you can finish hard projects.

Core Skills

Linux + systems debugging
Operate and debug production-like systems
Distributed systems
Scale training/inference across machines
Docker/Kubernetes
Run reproducible services and training jobs
Performance profiling
Find bottlenecks in code and infrastructure
Additional cross-cutting skills for this role: - Research writing: explain motivation, method, results, limitations, and next steps. - Experiment design: avoid misleading conclusions from noisy results. - Communication: make technical work understandable to teammates and stakeholders. - AI-assisted workflow: use AI to move faster, but verify everything important.

Proof of Work Projects

Project Level Time Expected Output
Model-serving API with load test Intermediate 3–6 weeks API + latency report
Distributed training toy demo Advanced 6–10 weeks Repo + writeup
Observability dashboard Intermediate 3–6 weeks Dashboard + metrics
A strong project for this role should include: 1. A clear problem statement. 2. A reproducible GitHub repository. 3. A short writeup explaining what you tried. 4. Results, screenshots, logs, benchmarks, or evaluation data. 5. A section on limitations and what you would improve next.

What AI Automation Replaces

  • Boilerplate code generation.
  • First drafts of documentation and reports.
  • Literature and documentation summaries.
  • Simple debugging suggestions.
  • Test case generation.
  • Plot/table generation.
  • Basic data cleaning or log summarization.
  • Scaffolding for APIs, dashboards, or experiments.

Why Humans Matter

  • Choosing the right problem is harder than generating code.
  • AI-generated experiments can be misleading without human judgment.
  • Safety, correctness, and reliability require accountability.
  • Real systems fail in messy ways that need context and ownership.
  • Research taste and product taste are difficult to automate fully.
  • Cross-team trust, prioritization, and communication remain human-heavy.
  • Someone must decide what evidence is strong enough to act on.

Performance Engineer

Prepare: 4–8+ years
Salary: $280k–$850k USD
Risk: Low

Optimizes throughput, robustness, sampling, load balancing, distributed systems, and low-level bottlenecks for large-scale ML. In practical terms, this role is about turning ambiguous AI problems into working systems, experiments, evaluations, tools, or research artifacts. It is not only about knowing AI concepts; it requires building, measuring, debugging, writing, and making decisions under uncertainty. For a student, the most important thing to understand is that this role rewards proof of ability: projects, research notes, reproducible experiments, open-source contributions, clear writing, and evidence that you can work through hard problems without needing perfect instructions.

Roles like this exist because advanced AI systems are becoming large, complex, and socially important. They need people who can improve capability while also thinking about reliability, safety, correctness, interpretability, performance, and real-world impact. AI can help with code and analysis, but high-stakes AI work still needs humans who can decide what to measure, which failures matter, and what should be trusted. By 2050, many routine tasks inside this role may be automated. The role may still remain valuable because the hard part is not only generating code or text; it is choosing good questions, designing robust systems, interpreting results, and taking responsibility for outcomes.

What you do

  • Translate ambiguous research/product/systems goals into concrete technical work.
  • Build experiments, infrastructure, evaluations, dashboards, tools, or model pipelines.
  • Debug failures across models, data, infrastructure, and human workflows.
  • Communicate findings through technical writing, design docs, reports, or presentations.
  • Collaborate with researchers, engineers, product teams, safety teams, or external experts.
  • Maintain high standards for reliability, reproducibility, and responsible deployment.
  • Use AI tools as accelerators while still verifying outputs and making final judgments.

Preparation Timeline

Month 0–3 — Foundations
Learn Python, Git/GitHub, Linux CLI, basic math, and how to read technical documentation. Build 2–3 small public repositories.
Month 3–6 — Applied ML and systems basics
Learn ML fundamentals, model evaluation, APIs, data handling, and basic backend or notebook workflows. Build one reproducible ML/evaluation project.
Month 6–12 — Role-specific projects
Pick one subfield and build a focused project: benchmark, dashboard, model-serving app, RL environment, safety classifier, interpretability notebook, or data pipeline.
Month 12–24 — Public proof of ability
Reproduce a small paper/blog result, publish a technical writeup, improve the project quality, and create a portfolio page with clear motivation, method, results, and limitations.
Beyond 24 months — Specialization
Deepen one track through internships, open-source work, research collaborations, production projects, or advanced systems/research projects.

Reality Check

This role is competitive. It is usually not an immediate beginner role unless it is explicitly an internship or fellowship. A first-year student should treat it as a long-term target and build toward it through small public projects, then larger reproducible projects, then collaborations, internships, research-style writing, or production-quality systems. Do not only collect certificates. For this kind of role, visible proof matters more: GitHub repositories, demos, benchmarks, technical writeups, open-source contributions, and evidence that you can finish hard projects.

Core Skills

CUDA/Triton basics
Write and optimize kernels
GPU architecture
Understand memory bandwidth, tensor cores, and occupancy
Profiling tools
Use Nsight/profilers to locate bottlenecks
Low precision inference
Understand FP8/INT8, quantization, and numerical tradeoffs
Additional cross-cutting skills for this role: - Research writing: explain motivation, method, results, limitations, and next steps. - Experiment design: avoid misleading conclusions from noisy results. - Communication: make technical work understandable to teammates and stakeholders. - AI-assisted workflow: use AI to move faster, but verify everything important.

Proof of Work Projects

Project Level Time Expected Output
CUDA matrix multiplication Intermediate 3–6 weeks Kernel + benchmark
Triton attention microbenchmark Advanced 4–8 weeks Benchmark + report
Quantized inference experiment Advanced 4–8 weeks Accuracy/latency analysis
A strong project for this role should include: 1. A clear problem statement. 2. A reproducible GitHub repository. 3. A short writeup explaining what you tried. 4. Results, screenshots, logs, benchmarks, or evaluation data. 5. A section on limitations and what you would improve next.

What AI Automation Replaces

  • Boilerplate code generation.
  • First drafts of documentation and reports.
  • Literature and documentation summaries.
  • Simple debugging suggestions.
  • Test case generation.
  • Plot/table generation.
  • Basic data cleaning or log summarization.
  • Scaffolding for APIs, dashboards, or experiments.

Why Humans Matter

  • Choosing the right problem is harder than generating code.
  • AI-generated experiments can be misleading without human judgment.
  • Safety, correctness, and reliability require accountability.
  • Real systems fail in messy ways that need context and ownership.
  • Research taste and product taste are difficult to automate fully.
  • Cross-team trust, prioritization, and communication remain human-heavy.
  • Someone must decide what evidence is strong enough to act on.

Best Next Step

Pick one small project from the table above. Build it in public. Write a clear README with: problem, method, results, limitations, and next steps. Then improve it once based on your own critique.

Performance Engineer, Inference Systems

Prepare: 4–8+ years
Salary: $350k–$850k USD
Risk: Low

Analyzes inference fleet throughput, latency, reliability, correctness, routing, batching, autoscaling, and hardware regressions. In practical terms, this role is about turning ambiguous AI problems into working systems, experiments, evaluations, tools, or research artifacts. It is not only about knowing AI concepts; it requires building, measuring, debugging, writing, and making decisions under uncertainty. For a student, the most important thing to understand is that this role rewards proof of ability: projects, research notes, reproducible experiments, open-source contributions, clear writing, and evidence that you can work through hard problems without needing perfect instructions.

Roles like this exist because advanced AI systems are becoming large, complex, and socially important. They need people who can improve capability while also thinking about reliability, safety, correctness, interpretability, performance, and real-world impact. AI can help with code and analysis, but high-stakes AI work still needs humans who can decide what to measure, which failures matter, and what should be trusted. By 2050, many routine tasks inside this role may be automated. The role may still remain valuable because the hard part is not only generating code or text; it is choosing good questions, designing robust systems, interpreting results, and taking responsibility for outcomes.

What you do

  • Translate ambiguous research/product/systems goals into concrete technical work.
  • Build experiments, infrastructure, evaluations, dashboards, tools, or model pipelines.
  • Debug failures across models, data, infrastructure, and human workflows.
  • Communicate findings through technical writing, design docs, reports, or presentations.
  • Collaborate with researchers, engineers, product teams, safety teams, or external experts.
  • Maintain high standards for reliability, reproducibility, and responsible deployment.
  • Use AI tools as accelerators while still verifying outputs and making final judgments.

Preparation Timeline

Month 0–3 — Foundations
Learn Python, Git/GitHub, Linux CLI, basic math, and how to read technical documentation. Build 2–3 small public repositories.
Month 3–6 — Applied ML and systems basics
Learn ML fundamentals, model evaluation, APIs, data handling, and basic backend or notebook workflows. Build one reproducible ML/evaluation project.
Month 6–12 — Role-specific projects
Pick one subfield and build a focused project: benchmark, dashboard, model-serving app, RL environment, safety classifier, interpretability notebook, or data pipeline.
Month 12–24 — Public proof of ability
Reproduce a small paper/blog result, publish a technical writeup, improve the project quality, and create a portfolio page with clear motivation, method, results, and limitations.
Beyond 24 months — Specialization
Deepen one track through internships, open-source work, research collaborations, production projects, or advanced systems/research projects.

Reality Check

This role is competitive. It is usually not an immediate beginner role unless it is explicitly an internship or fellowship. A first-year student should treat it as a long-term target and build toward it through small public projects, then larger reproducible projects, then collaborations, internships, research-style writing, or production-quality systems. Do not only collect certificates. For this kind of role, visible proof matters more: GitHub repositories, demos, benchmarks, technical writeups, open-source contributions, and evidence that you can finish hard projects.

Core Skills

CUDA/Triton basics
Write and optimize kernels
GPU architecture
Understand memory bandwidth, tensor cores, and occupancy
Profiling tools
Use Nsight/profilers to locate bottlenecks
Low precision inference
Understand FP8/INT8, quantization, and numerical tradeoffs
Additional cross-cutting skills for this role: - Research writing: explain motivation, method, results, limitations, and next steps. - Experiment design: avoid misleading conclusions from noisy results. - Communication: make technical work understandable to teammates and stakeholders. - AI-assisted workflow: use AI to move faster, but verify everything important.

Proof of Work Projects

Project Level Time Expected Output
CUDA matrix multiplication Intermediate 3–6 weeks Kernel + benchmark
Triton attention microbenchmark Advanced 4–8 weeks Benchmark + report
Quantized inference experiment Advanced 4–8 weeks Accuracy/latency analysis
A strong project for this role should include: 1. A clear problem statement. 2. A reproducible GitHub repository. 3. A short writeup explaining what you tried. 4. Results, screenshots, logs, benchmarks, or evaluation data. 5. A section on limitations and what you would improve next.

What AI Automation Replaces

  • Boilerplate code generation.
  • First drafts of documentation and reports.
  • Literature and documentation summaries.
  • Simple debugging suggestions.
  • Test case generation.
  • Plot/table generation.
  • Basic data cleaning or log summarization.
  • Scaffolding for APIs, dashboards, or experiments.

Why Humans Matter

  • Choosing the right problem is harder than generating code.
  • AI-generated experiments can be misleading without human judgment.
  • Safety, correctness, and reliability require accountability.
  • Real systems fail in messy ways that need context and ownership.
  • Research taste and product taste are difficult to automate fully.
  • Cross-team trust, prioritization, and communication remain human-heavy.
  • Someone must decide what evidence is strong enough to act on.

Best Next Step

Pick one small project from the table above. Build it in public. Write a clear README with: problem, method, results, limitations, and next steps. Then improve it once based on your own critique.

Official Source Links

  • Official Anthropic job board
  • Anthropic careers
  • Candidate AI usage guidance
  • Alignment Science blog
  • Frontier Red Team blog
  • Transformer Circuits
Back to Directory

Research Engineer, Pretraining

Prepare: 3–8+ years
Salary: £260k–£630k GBP
Risk: Low

Contributes to model architecture, algorithms, data processing, optimizer development, distributed training, and pretraining infrastructure. In practical terms, this role is about turning ambiguous AI problems into working systems, experiments, evaluations, tools, or research artifacts. It is not only about knowing AI concepts; it requires building, measuring, debugging, writing, and making decisions under uncertainty. For a student, the most important thing to understand is that this role rewards proof of ability: projects, research notes, reproducible experiments, open-source contributions, clear writing, and evidence that you can work through hard problems without needing perfect instructions.

Roles like this exist because advanced AI systems are becoming large, complex, and socially important. They need people who can improve capability while also thinking about reliability, safety, correctness, interpretability, performance, and real-world impact. AI can help with code and analysis, but high-stakes AI work still needs humans who can decide what to measure, which failures matter, and what should be trusted. By 2050, many routine tasks inside this role may be automated. The role may still remain valuable because the hard part is not only generating code or text; it is choosing good questions, designing robust systems, interpreting results, and taking responsibility for outcomes.

What you do

  • Translate ambiguous research/product/systems goals into concrete technical work.
  • Build experiments, infrastructure, evaluations, dashboards, tools, or model pipelines.
  • Debug failures across models, data, infrastructure, and human workflows.
  • Communicate findings through technical writing, design docs, reports, or presentations.
  • Collaborate with researchers, engineers, product teams, safety teams, or external experts.
  • Maintain high standards for reliability, reproducibility, and responsible deployment.
  • Use AI tools as accelerators while still verifying outputs and making final judgments.

Preparation Timeline

Month 0–3 — Foundations
Learn Python, Git/GitHub, Linux CLI, basic math, and how to read technical documentation. Build 2–3 small public repositories.
Month 3–6 — Applied ML and systems basics
Learn ML fundamentals, model evaluation, APIs, data handling, and basic backend or notebook workflows. Build one reproducible ML/evaluation project.
Month 6–12 — Role-specific projects
Pick one subfield and build a focused project: benchmark, dashboard, model-serving app, RL environment, safety classifier, interpretability notebook, or data pipeline.
Month 12–24 — Public proof of ability
Reproduce a small paper/blog result, publish a technical writeup, improve the project quality, and create a portfolio page with clear motivation, method, results, and limitations.
Beyond 24 months — Specialization
Deepen one track through internships, open-source work, research collaborations, production projects, or advanced systems/research projects.

Reality Check

This role is competitive. It is usually not an immediate beginner role unless it is explicitly an internship or fellowship. A first-year student should treat it as a long-term target and build toward it through small public projects, then larger reproducible projects, then collaborations, internships, research-style writing, or production-quality systems. Do not only collect certificates. For this kind of role, visible proof matters more: GitHub repositories, demos, benchmarks, technical writeups, open-source contributions, and evidence that you can finish hard projects.

Core Skills

Linux + systems debugging
Operate and debug production-like systems
Distributed systems
Scale training/inference across machines
Docker/Kubernetes
Run reproducible services and training jobs
Performance profiling
Find bottlenecks in code and infrastructure
Additional cross-cutting skills for this role: - Research writing: explain motivation, method, results, limitations, and next steps. - Experiment design: avoid misleading conclusions from noisy results. - Communication: make technical work understandable to teammates and stakeholders. - AI-assisted workflow: use AI to move faster, but verify everything important.

Proof of Work Projects

Project Level Time Expected Output
Model-serving API with load test Intermediate 3–6 weeks API + latency report
Distributed training toy demo Advanced 6–10 weeks Repo + writeup
Observability dashboard Intermediate 3–6 weeks Dashboard + metrics
A strong project for this role should include: 1. A clear problem statement. 2. A reproducible GitHub repository. 3. A short writeup explaining what you tried. 4. Results, screenshots, logs, benchmarks, or evaluation data. 5. A section on limitations and what you would improve next.

What AI Automation Replaces

  • Boilerplate code generation.
  • First drafts of documentation and reports.
  • Literature and documentation summaries.
  • Simple debugging suggestions.
  • Test case generation.
  • Plot/table generation.
  • Basic data cleaning or log summarization.
  • Scaffolding for APIs, dashboards, or experiments.

Why Humans Matter

  • Choosing the right problem is harder than generating code.
  • AI-generated experiments can be misleading without human judgment.
  • Safety, correctness, and reliability require accountability.
  • Real systems fail in messy ways that need context and ownership.
  • Research taste and product taste are difficult to automate fully.
  • Cross-team trust, prioritization, and communication remain human-heavy.
  • Someone must decide what evidence is strong enough to act on.

Best Next Step

Pick one small project from the table above. Build it in public. Write a clear README with: problem, method, results, limitations, and next steps. Then improve it once based on your own critique.

Research Engineer, Pretraining Scaling

Prepare: 4–8+ years
Salary: $350k–$850k USD / £260k–£630k GBP

Ensures frontier pretraining runs are reliable, efficient, observable, and scalable during high-pressure model launches. Research Engineer / Research Scientist, Pre-training

Prepare: 3–8+ years
Salary: $350k–$850k USD / £260k–£630k GBP / CHF280k–CHF680k

Develops next-generation LLMs and multimodal capabilities through architecture, data processing, optimizers, and large-scale experiments. In practical terms, this role is about turning ambiguous AI problems into working systems, experiments, evaluations, tools, or research artifacts. It is not only about knowing AI concepts; it requires building, measuring, debugging, writing, and making decisions under uncertainty. For a student, the most important thing to understand is that this role rewards proof of ability: projects, research notes, reproducible experiments, open-source contributions, clear writing, and evidence that you can work through hard problems without needing perfect instructions.

Roles like this exist because advanced AI systems are becoming large, complex, and socially important. They need people who can improve capability while also thinking about reliability, safety, correctness, interpretability, performance, and real-world impact. AI can help with code and analysis, but high-stakes AI work still needs humans who can decide what to measure, which failures matter, and what should be trusted. Research Engineer / Research Scientist, Tokens

Prepare: 3–7+ years
Salary: $350k–$500k USD
Risk: Low

Broad large-scale ML systems role touching training throughput, ETL, experiments, tooling, transformers, and RL. In practical terms, this role is about turning ambiguous AI problems into working systems, experiments, evaluations, tools, or research artifacts. It is not only about knowing AI concepts; it requires building, measuring, debugging, writing, and making decisions under uncertainty. For a student, the most important thing to understand is that this role rewards proof of ability: projects, research notes, reproducible experiments, open-source contributions, clear writing, and evidence that you can work through hard problems without needing perfect instructions.

Roles like this exist because advanced AI systems are becoming large, complex, and socially important. They need people who can improve capability while also thinking about reliability, safety, correctness, interpretability, performance, and real-world impact. AI can help with code and analysis, but high-stakes AI work still needs humans who can decide what to measure, which failures matter, and what should be trusted. By 2050, many routine tasks inside this role may be automated. The role may still remain valuable because the hard part is not only generating code or text; it is choosing good questions, designing robust systems, interpreting results, and taking responsibility for outcomes.

What you do

  • Translate ambiguous research/product/systems goals into concrete technical work.
  • Build experiments, infrastructure, evaluations, dashboards, tools, or model pipelines.
  • Debug failures across models, data, infrastructure, and human workflows.
  • Communicate findings through technical writing, design docs, reports, or presentations.
  • Collaborate with researchers, engineers, product teams, safety teams, or external experts.
  • Maintain high standards for reliability, reproducibility, and responsible deployment.
  • Use AI tools as accelerators while still verifying outputs and making final judgments.

Preparation Timeline

Month 0–3 — Foundations
Learn Python, Git/GitHub, Linux CLI, basic math, and how to read technical documentation. Build 2–3 small public repositories.
Month 3–6 — Applied ML and systems basics
Learn ML fundamentals, model evaluation, APIs, data handling, and basic backend or notebook workflows. Build one reproducible ML/evaluation project.
Month 6–12 — Role-specific projects
Pick one subfield and build a focused project: benchmark, dashboard, model-serving app, RL environment, safety classifier, interpretability notebook, or data pipeline.
Month 12–24 — Public proof of ability
Reproduce a small paper/blog result, publish a technical writeup, improve the project quality, and create a portfolio page with clear motivation, method, results, and limitations.
Beyond 24 months — Specialization
Deepen one track through internships, open-source work, research collaborations, production projects, or advanced systems/research projects.

Reality Check

This role is competitive. It is usually not an immediate beginner role unless it is explicitly an internship or fellowship. A first-year student should treat it as a long-term target and build toward it through small public projects, then larger reproducible projects, then collaborations, internships, research-style writing, or production-quality systems. Do not only collect certificates. For this kind of role, visible proof matters more: GitHub repositories, demos, benchmarks, technical writeups, open-source contributions, and evidence that you can finish hard projects.

Core Skills

Python + PyTorch/JAX
Implement and inspect model internals
Transformer circuits
Understand attention heads, residual streams, features, superposition
Activation analysis
Extract and intervene on activations
Research tooling
Build tools researchers can use repeatedly
Additional cross-cutting skills for this role: - Research writing: explain motivation, method, results, limitations, and next steps. - Experiment design: avoid misleading conclusions from noisy results. - Communication: make technical work understandable to teammates and stakeholders. - AI-assisted workflow: use AI to move faster, but verify everything important.

Proof of Work Projects

Project Level Time Expected Output
Activation patching notebook Intermediate 3–6 weeks Notebook + explanation
Feature visualization mini-tool Intermediate 4–8 weeks Tool + case study
Transformer circuits reproduction Advanced 6–12 weeks Repo + writeup
A strong project for this role should include: 1. A clear problem statement. 2. A reproducible GitHub repository. 3. A short writeup explaining what you tried. 4. Results, screenshots, logs, benchmarks, or evaluation data. 5. A section on limitations and what you would improve next.

What AI Automation Replaces

  • Boilerplate code generation.
  • First drafts of documentation and reports.
  • Literature and documentation summaries.
  • Simple debugging suggestions.
  • Test case generation.
  • Plot/table generation.
  • Basic data cleaning or log summarization.
  • Scaffolding for APIs, dashboards, or experiments.

Why Humans Matter

  • Choosing the right problem is harder than generating code.
  • AI-generated experiments can be misleading without human judgment.
  • Safety, correctness, and reliability require accountability.
  • Real systems fail in messy ways that need context and ownership.
  • Research taste and product taste are difficult to automate fully.
  • Cross-team trust, prioritization, and communication remain human-heavy.
  • Someone must decide what evidence is strong enough to act on.

Research Engineer, Science of Scaling

Prepare: 3–8+ years

Studies how compute turns into intelligence through scaling experiments, algorithms, infrastructure optimization, and large-scale ML research.   • Someone must decide what evidence is strong enough to act on.

Best Next Step

Pick one small project from the table above. Build it in public. Write a clear README with: problem, method, results, limitations, and next steps. Then improve it once based on your own critique.

Official Source Links

  • Official Anthropic job board
  • Anthropic careers
  • Candidate AI usage guidance
  • Alignment Science blog
  • Frontier Red Team blog
  • Transformer Circuits
Back to Directory

Product Engineer, Computer Use

Prepare: 2–5+ years
Salary: $300k–$320k USD
Risk: Low–Medium

Builds computer-use and browser-control product surfaces, agent runtimes, reliability systems, and end-user AI agent workflows. In practical terms, this role is about turning ambiguous AI problems into working systems, experiments, evaluations, tools, or research artifacts. It is not only about knowing AI concepts; it requires building, measuring, debugging, writing, and making decisions under uncertainty. For a student, the most important thing to understand is that this role rewards proof of ability: projects, research notes, reproducible experiments, open-source contributions, clear writing, and evidence that you can work through hard problems without needing perfect instructions.

Roles like this exist because advanced AI systems are becoming large, complex, and socially important. They need people who can improve capability while also thinking about reliability, safety, correctness, interpretability, performance, and real-world impact. AI can help with code and analysis, but high-stakes AI work still needs humans who can decide what to measure, which failures matter, and what should be trusted. By 2050, many routine tasks inside this role may be automated. The role may still remain valuable because the hard part is not only generating code or text; it is choosing good questions, designing robust systems, interpreting results, and taking responsibility for outcomes.

What you do

  • Translate ambiguous research/product/systems goals into concrete technical work.
  • Build experiments, infrastructure, evaluations, dashboards, tools, or model pipelines.
  • Debug failures across models, data, infrastructure, and human workflows.
  • Communicate findings through technical writing, design docs, reports, or presentations.
  • Collaborate with researchers, engineers, product teams, safety teams, or external experts.
  • Maintain high standards for reliability, reproducibility, and responsible deployment.
  • Use AI tools as accelerators while still verifying outputs and making final judgments.

Preparation Timeline

Month 0–3 — Foundations
Learn Python, Git/GitHub, Linux CLI, basic math, and how to read technical documentation. Build 2–3 small public repositories.
Month 3–6 — Applied ML and systems basics
Learn ML fundamentals, model evaluation, APIs, data handling, and basic backend or notebook workflows. Build one reproducible ML/evaluation project.
Month 6–12 — Role-specific projects
Pick one subfield and build a focused project: benchmark, dashboard, model-serving app, RL environment, safety classifier, interpretability notebook, or data pipeline.
Month 12–24 — Public proof of ability
Reproduce a small paper/blog result, publish a technical writeup, improve the project quality, and create a portfolio page with clear motivation, method, results, and limitations.
Beyond 24 months — Specialization
Deepen one track through internships, open-source work, research collaborations, production projects, or advanced systems/research projects.

Reality Check

This role is competitive. It is usually not an immediate beginner role unless it is explicitly an internship or fellowship. A first-year student should treat it as a long-term target and build toward it through small public projects, then larger reproducible projects, then collaborations, internships, research-style writing, or production-quality systems. Do not only collect certificates. For this kind of role, visible proof matters more: GitHub repositories, demos, benchmarks, technical writeups, open-source contributions, and evidence that you can finish hard projects.

Core Skills

Full-stack engineering
Own product from database to interface
React/TypeScript
Build polished user-facing tools
Product judgment
Decide what to build and what to cut
LLM APIs/agent frameworks
Build AI-native experiences
Additional cross-cutting skills for this role: - Research writing: explain motivation, method, results, limitations, and next steps. - Experiment design: avoid misleading conclusions from noisy results. - Communication: make technical work understandable to teammates and stakeholders. - AI-assisted workflow: use AI to move faster, but verify everything important.

Proof of Work Projects

Project Level Time Expected Output
AI workflow product Intermediate 3–6 weeks Deployed app + case study
Research dashboard Intermediate 3–6 weeks Dashboard + users
Browser-control agent prototype Advanced 6–10 weeks Agent + eval notes
A strong project for this role should include: 1. A clear problem statement. 2. A reproducible GitHub repository. 3. A short writeup explaining what you tried. 4. Results, screenshots, logs, benchmarks, or evaluation data. 5. A section on limitations and what you would improve next.

What AI Automation Replaces

  • Boilerplate code generation.
  • First drafts of documentation and reports.
  • Literature and documentation summaries.
  • Simple debugging suggestions.
  • Test case generation.
  • Plot/table generation.
  • Basic data cleaning or log summarization.
  • Scaffolding for APIs, dashboards, or experiments.

Why Humans Matter

  • Choosing the right problem is harder than generating code.
  • AI-generated experiments can be misleading without human judgment.
  • Safety, correctness, and reliability require accountability.
  • Real systems fail in messy ways that need context and ownership.
  • Research taste and product taste are difficult to automate fully.
  • Cross-team trust, prioritization, and communication remain human-heavy.
  • Someone must decide what evidence is strong enough to act on.

Best Next Step

Pick one small project from the table above. Build it in public. Write a clear README with: problem, method, results, limitations, and next steps. Then improve it once based on your own critique.

Official Source Links

  • Official Anthropic job board
  • Anthropic careers
  • Candidate AI usage guidance
  • Alignment Science blog
  • Frontier Red Team blog
  • Transformer Circuits
Back to Directory

Research Engineer, AI Observability

Prepare: 3–6+ years
Salary: $320k–$405k USD
Risk: Low

Builds AI-based monitoring systems that analyze large unstructured datasets and maintain human oversight over AI training/deployment. In practical terms, this role is about turning ambiguous AI problems into working systems, experiments, evaluations, tools, or research artifacts. It is not only about knowing AI concepts; it requires building, measuring, debugging, writing, and making decisions under uncertainty. For a student, the most important thing to understand is that this role rewards proof of ability: projects, research notes, reproducible experiments, open-source contributions, clear writing, and evidence that you can work through hard problems without needing perfect instructions.

Roles like this exist because advanced AI systems are becoming large, complex, and socially important. They need people who can improve capability while also thinking about reliability, safety, correctness, interpretability, performance, and real-world impact. AI can help with code and analysis, but high-stakes AI work still needs humans who can decide what to measure, which failures matter, and what should be trusted. By 2050, many routine tasks inside this role may be automated. The role may still remain valuable because the hard part is not only generating code or text; it is choosing good questions, designing robust systems, interpreting results, and taking responsibility for outcomes.

What you do

  • Translate ambiguous research/product/systems goals into concrete technical work.
  • Build experiments, infrastructure, evaluations, dashboards, tools, or model pipelines.
  • Debug failures across models, data, infrastructure, and human workflows.
  • Communicate findings through technical writing, design docs, reports, or presentations.
  • Collaborate with researchers, engineers, product teams, safety teams, or external experts.
  • Maintain high standards for reliability, reproducibility, and responsible deployment.
  • Use AI tools as accelerators while still verifying outputs and making final judgments.

Preparation Timeline

Month 0–3 — Foundations
Learn Python, Git/GitHub, Linux CLI, basic math, and how to read technical documentation. Build 2–3 small public repositories.
Month 3–6 — Applied ML and systems basics
Learn ML fundamentals, model evaluation, APIs, data handling, and basic backend or notebook workflows. Build one reproducible ML/evaluation project.
Month 6–12 — Role-specific projects
Pick one subfield and build a focused project: benchmark, dashboard, model-serving app, RL environment, safety classifier, interpretability notebook, or data pipeline.
Month 12–24 — Public proof of ability
Reproduce a small paper/blog result, publish a technical writeup, improve the project quality, and create a portfolio page with clear motivation, method, results, and limitations.
Beyond 24 months — Specialization
Deepen one track through internships, open-source work, research collaborations, production projects, or advanced systems/research projects.

Reality Check

This role is competitive. It is usually not an immediate beginner role unless it is explicitly an internship or fellowship. A first-year student should treat it as a long-term target and build toward it through small public projects, then larger reproducible projects, then collaborations, internships, research-style writing, or production-quality systems. Do not only collect certificates. For this kind of role, visible proof matters more: GitHub repositories, demos, benchmarks, technical writeups, open-source contributions, and evidence that you can finish hard projects.

Core Skills

Python data stack
pandas, SQL, APIs, data processing
Data pipelines
Build robust ingestion and transformation workflows
Privacy/governance basics
Handle sensitive research data responsibly
Visualization/writing
Communicate evidence clearly
Additional cross-cutting skills for this role: - Research writing: explain motivation, method, results, limitations, and next steps. - Experiment design: avoid misleading conclusions from noisy results. - Communication: make technical work understandable to teammates and stakeholders. - AI-assisted workflow: use AI to move faster, but verify everything important.

Proof of Work Projects

Project Level Time Expected Output
Public dataset analysis Beginner–Intermediate 2–4 weeks Notebook + report
ETL pipeline Intermediate 3–6 weeks Pipeline + docs
Research data API Advanced 4–8 weeks API + dashboard
A strong project for this role should include: 1. A clear problem statement. 2. A reproducible GitHub repository. 3. A short writeup explaining what you tried. 4. Results, screenshots, logs, benchmarks, or evaluation data. 5. A section on limitations and what you would improve next.

What AI Automation Replaces

  • Boilerplate code generation.
  • First drafts of documentation and reports.
  • Literature and documentation summaries.
  • Simple debugging suggestions.
  • Test case generation.
  • Plot/table generation.
  • Basic data cleaning or log summarization.
  • Scaffolding for APIs, dashboards, or experiments.

Why Humans Matter

  • Choosing the right problem is harder than generating code.
  • AI-generated experiments can be misleading without human judgment.
  • Safety, correctness, and reliability require accountability.
  • Real systems fail in messy ways that need context and ownership.
  • Research taste and product taste are difficult to automate fully.
  • Cross-team trust, prioritization, and communication remain human-heavy.
  • Someone must decide what evidence is strong enough to act on.

Best Next Step

Pick one small project from the table above. Build it in public. Write a clear README with: problem, method, results, limitations, and next steps. Then improve it once based on your own critique.

Research Engineer, Discovery

Prepare: 4–8+ years
Salary: $350k–$850k USD
Risk: Low

Builds infrastructure for an AI scientist: large-scale evaluation, sandboxing, distributed environments, data pipelines, and scientific workflows. In practical terms, this role is about turning ambiguous AI problems into working systems, experiments, evaluations, tools, or research artifacts. It is not only about knowing AI concepts; it requires building, measuring, debugging, writing, and making decisions under uncertainty. For a student, the most important thing to understand is that this role rewards proof of ability: projects, research notes, reproducible experiments, open-source contributions, clear writing, and evidence that you can work through hard problems without needing perfect instructions.

Roles like this exist because advanced AI systems are becoming large, complex, and socially important. They need people who can improve capability while also thinking about reliability, safety, correctness, interpretability, performance, and real-world impact. AI can help with code and analysis, but high-stakes AI work still needs humans who can decide what to measure, which failures matter, and what should be trusted. By 2050, many routine tasks inside this role may be automated. The role may still remain valuable because the hard part is not only generating code or text; it is choosing good questions, designing robust systems, interpreting results, and taking responsibility for outcomes.

What you do

  • Translate ambiguous research/product/systems goals into concrete technical work.
  • Build experiments, infrastructure, evaluations, dashboards, tools, or model pipelines.
  • Debug failures across models, data, infrastructure, and human workflows.
  • Communicate findings through technical writing, design docs, reports, or presentations.
  • Collaborate with researchers, engineers, product teams, safety teams, or external experts.
  • Maintain high standards for reliability, reproducibility, and responsible deployment.
  • Use AI tools as accelerators while still verifying outputs and making final judgments.

Preparation Timeline

Month 0–3 — Foundations
Learn Python, Git/GitHub, Linux CLI, basic math, and how to read technical documentation. Build 2–3 small public repositories.
Month 3–6 — Applied ML and systems basics
Learn ML fundamentals, model evaluation, APIs, data handling, and basic backend or notebook workflows. Build one reproducible ML/evaluation project.
Month 6–12 — Role-specific projects
Pick one subfield and build a focused project: benchmark, dashboard, model-serving app, RL environment, safety classifier, interpretability notebook, or data pipeline.
Month 12–24 — Public proof of ability
Reproduce a small paper/blog result, publish a technical writeup, improve the project quality, and create a portfolio page with clear motivation, method, results, and limitations.
Beyond 24 months — Specialization
Deepen one track through internships, open-source work, research collaborations, production projects, or advanced systems/research projects.

Reality Check

This role is competitive. It is usually not an immediate beginner role unless it is explicitly an internship or fellowship. A first-year student should treat it as a long-term target and build toward it through small public projects, then larger reproducible projects, then collaborations, internships, research-style writing, or production-quality systems. Do not only collect certificates. For this kind of role, visible proof matters more: GitHub repositories, demos, benchmarks, technical writeups, open-source contributions, and evidence that you can finish hard projects.

Core Skills

Linux + systems debugging
Operate and debug production-like systems
Distributed systems
Scale training/inference across machines
Docker/Kubernetes
Run reproducible services and training jobs
Performance profiling
Find bottlenecks in code and infrastructure
Additional cross-cutting skills for this role: - Research writing: explain motivation, method, results, limitations, and next steps. - Experiment design: avoid misleading conclusions from noisy results. - Communication: make technical work understandable to teammates and stakeholders. - AI-assisted workflow: use AI to move faster, but verify everything important.

Proof of Work Projects

Project Level Time Expected Output
Model-serving API with load test Intermediate 3–6 weeks API + latency report
Distributed training toy demo Advanced 6–10 weeks Repo + writeup
Observability dashboard Intermediate 3–6 weeks Dashboard + metrics
A strong project for this role should include: 1. A clear problem statement. 2. A reproducible GitHub repository. 3. A short writeup explaining what you tried. 4. Results, screenshots, logs, benchmarks, or evaluation data. 5. A section on limitations and what you would improve next.

What AI Automation Replaces

  • Boilerplate code generation.
  • First drafts of documentation and reports.
  • Literature and documentation summaries.
  • Simple debugging suggestions.
  • Test case generation.
  • Plot/table generation.
  • Basic data cleaning or log summarization.
  • Scaffolding for APIs, dashboards, or experiments.

Why Humans Matter

  • Choosing the right problem is harder than generating code.
  • AI-generated experiments can be misleading without human judgment.
  • Safety, correctness, and reliability require accountability.
  • Real systems fail in messy ways that need context and ownership.
  • Research taste and product taste are difficult to automate fully.
  • Cross-team trust, prioritization, and communication remain human-heavy.
  • Someone must decide what evidence is strong enough to act on.

Research Engineer, Knowledge Foundations

Prepare: 3–7+ years
Salary: $350k–$850k USD
Risk: Low

Improves how Claude searches, retrieves, reasons over information, and handles knowledge-intensive tasks through RL environments and evals. In practical terms, this role is about turning ambiguous AI problems into working systems, experiments, evaluations, tools, or research artifacts. It is not only about knowing AI concepts; it requires building, measuring, debugging, writing, and making decisions under uncertainty. For a student, the most important thing to understand is that this role rewards proof of ability: projects, research notes, reproducible experiments, open-source contributions, clear writing, and evidence that you can work through hard problems without needing perfect instructions.

Roles like this exist because advanced AI systems are becoming large, complex, and socially important. They need people who can improve capability while also thinking about reliability, safety, correctness, interpretability, performance, and real-world impact. AI can help with code and analysis, but high-stakes AI work still needs humans who can decide what to measure, which failures matter, and what should be trusted.
